The **Czech Republic**,[[e]](#cite_note-19)[[15]](#cite_note-20) also known as **Czechia**[[f]](#cite_note-21)[[16]](#cite_note-22) and historically known as **Bohemia**,[[17]](#cite_note-23) is a [landlocked country](/wiki/Landlocked_country "Landlocked country") in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"). The country is bordered by [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") to the south, [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") to the west, [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") to the northeast, and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") to the southeast.[[18]](#cite_note-24) The Czech Republic has a hilly landscape that covers an area of 78,871 square kilometers (30,452 sq mi) with a mostly temperate [continental](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") and [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"). The capital and largest city is [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"); other major cities and urban areas include [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"), [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava"), [Plzeň](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň") and [Liberec](/wiki/Liberec "Liberec").

The [Duchy of Bohemia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bohemia "Duchy of Bohemia") was founded in the late 9th century under [Great Moravia](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia"). It was formally recognized as an [Imperial Estate](/wiki/Imperial_Estate "Imperial Estate") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in 1002 and became [a kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia") in 1198.[[19]](#cite_note-The_Path-25)[[20]](#cite_note-26) Following the [Battle of Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs "Battle of Mohács") in 1526, all of the [Lands of the Bohemian Crown](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown "Lands of the Bohemian Crown") were gradually integrated into the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). Nearly a hundred years later, the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") [Bohemian Revolt](/wiki/Bohemian_Revolt "Bohemian Revolt") led to the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"). After the [Battle of White Mountain](/wiki/Battle_of_White_Mountain "Battle of White Mountain"), the Habsburgs consolidated their rule. With the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire in 1806, the Crown lands became part of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire").

During the 19th century, the [Czech lands](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands") underwent significant industrialization. Following the collapse of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), most of the region became part of the [First Czechoslovak Republic](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic") in 1918.[[21]](#cite_note-27) Czechoslovakia was the only country in Central and Eastern Europe to remain a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") during the entirety of the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period").[[22]](#cite_note-Ash0-28) After the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") in 1938, [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") systematically [took control](/wiki/Occupation_of_Czechoslovakia_(1938%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Czechoslovakia (1938–1945)") over the Czech lands. [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Third_Czechoslovak_Republic "Third Czechoslovak Republic") was restored in 1945 and three years later became an [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") state following a [coup d'état](/wiki/1948_Czechoslovak_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1948 Czechoslovak coup d'état") in 1948. Attempts to liberalize the government and economy were suppressed by a [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")-led invasion of the country during the [Prague Spring](/wiki/Prague_Spring "Prague Spring") in 1968. In November 1989, the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") ended communist rule in the country and restored [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"). On 31 December 1992, [Czechoslovakia was peacefully dissolved](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Czechoslovakia "Dissolution of Czechoslovakia"), with its constituent states becoming the independent states of the Czech Republic and Slovakia.

The Czech Republic is a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic") and [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with an [advanced](/wiki/Developed_country#IMF_advanced_economies "Developed country"), [high-income](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy") [social market economy](/wiki/Social_market_economy "Social market economy"). It is a [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") with a [European social model](/wiki/European_social_model "European social model"), [universal health care](/wiki/Healthcare_in_the_Czech_Republic "Healthcare in the Czech Republic") and [free-tuition](/wiki/Free_education "Free education") [university education](/wiki/Education_in_the_Czech_Republic#Tertiary_education "Education in the Czech Republic"). It ranks 32nd in the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"). The Czech Republic is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") and the [Visegrád Group](/wiki/Visegr%C3%A1d_Group "Visegrád Group").

## Etymology

Main article: [Name of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Name_of_the_Czech_Republic "Name of the Czech Republic")

The traditional English name "Bohemia" derives from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *Boiohaemum*, which means "home of the [Boii](/wiki/Boii "Boii")" (a [Gallic](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls") tribe). The current English name ultimately comes from the Czech word *Čech*.[[23]](#cite_note-29)[[24]](#cite_note-American_Heritage_Dictionary-30)[[25]](#cite_note-OxfordDictionary-31) The name comes from the Slavic tribe ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Češi, Čechové*) and, according to legend, their leader [Čech](/wiki/Lech,_Czech,_and_Rus%27 "Lech, Czech, and Rus'"), who brought them to Bohemia, to settle on [Říp](/wiki/%C5%98%C3%ADp "Říp"), a 461-metre high hill. The etymology of the word *Čech* is uncertain. According to the most common derivation, it can be traced back to the [Proto-Slavic](/wiki/Proto-Slavic_language "Proto-Slavic language") root \**čel-*, meaning 'member of the people; kinsman'. Thus, it is [cognate](/wiki/Cognate_(linguistics) "Cognate (linguistics)") to the Czech word *[člověk](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C4%8Dlov%C4%9Bk#Czech "wikt:člověk")* 'person'.[[26]](#cite_note-32)[[27]](#cite_note-Cmejrkova2010-33)[[28]](#cite_note-Dickins2011-34)[[29]](#cite_note-Rejzek2012-35)

The country has been traditionally divided into three lands, namely [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") (*Čechy*) in the west, [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") (*Morava*) in the east, and [Czech Silesia](/wiki/Czech_Silesia "Czech Silesia") (*Slezsko*; the smaller, south-eastern part of historical [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"), most of which is located within modern Poland) in the northeast.[[30]](#cite_note-36) Known as the *lands of the Bohemian Crown* since the 14th century, a number of other names for the country have been used, including *Czech/Bohemian lands*, *Bohemian Crown*, *Czechia*,[[31]](#cite_note-37) and the *lands of the Crown of [Saint Wenceslaus](/wiki/Wenceslaus_I,_Duke_of_Bohemia "Wenceslaus I, Duke of Bohemia")*. When the country regained its independence after the dissolution of the [Austro-Hungarian empire](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in 1918, the new name of *Czechoslovakia* was coined to reflect the union of the Czech and Slovak nations within one country.[[32]](#cite_note-Thompson2012-38)

After Czechoslovakia dissolved on the last day of 1992, *Česko* was adopted as the Czech short name for the new state and the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Czech_Republic) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Czech Republic)") recommended *Czechia* for the English-language equivalent.[[33]](#cite_note-39) This form was not widely adopted at the time, leading to the long name *Czech Republic* being used in English in nearly all circumstances. The Czech government [directed use of *Czechia*](/wiki/Name_of_the_Czech_Republic#Adoption_of_Czechia "Name of the Czech Republic") as the official English short name in 2016.[[34]](#cite_note-40) The short name has been listed by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")[[35]](#cite_note-41) and is used by other organizations such as the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"),[[36]](#cite_note-42) [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"),[[37]](#cite_note-43) the [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency"),[[38]](#cite_note-44) [Google Maps](/wiki/Google_Maps "Google Maps"),[[39]](#cite_note-45) and the [European Broadcasting Union](/wiki/European_Broadcasting_Union "European Broadcasting Union").[[40]](#cite_note-46) In 2022, the American *[AP Stylebook](/wiki/AP_Stylebook "AP Stylebook")* stated in its entry on the country that "both [Czechia and the Czech Republic] are acceptable. The shorter name Czechia is preferred by the Czech government. If using Czechia, clarify in the story that the country is more widely known in English as the Czech Republic."[[41]](#cite_note-47)

## History

Main article: [History of the Czech lands](/wiki/History_of_the_Czech_lands "History of the Czech lands")

### Prehistory

[![Ceramic sculpture](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Vestonicka_venuse_edit.jpg/120px-Vestonicka_venuse_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vestonicka_venuse_edit.jpg)

[![Stone Sculpture](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_stone_head_of_a_Celt.jpg/250px-The_stone_head_of_a_Celt.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_stone_head_of_a_Celt.jpg)

**Left**: [Venus of Dolní Věstonice](/wiki/Venus_of_Doln%C3%AD_V%C4%9Bstonice "Venus of Dolní Věstonice"), dated to 29,000–25,000 [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"), is the oldest ceramic figurine in the world.[[42]](#cite_note-48)  
**Right**: the [stone head of a Celt](/wiki/M%C5%A1eck%C3%A9_%C5%BDehrovice_Head "Mšecké Žehrovice Head") is the most valuable Celtic sculpture in Europe and dates to the 3rd century BCE.[[43]](#cite_note-49)

Archaeological evidence indicates that the area now known as the Czech Republic has been inhabited since the [Paleolithic era](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic"). Notably, the [Venus of Dolní Věstonice](/wiki/Venus_of_Doln%C3%AD_V%C4%9Bstonice "Venus of Dolní Věstonice"), a ceramic figurine dated to approximately 29,000–25,000 BCE, was discovered in this region.[[44]](#cite_note-50) This artifact is considered the oldest known ceramic figurine in the world.[[45]](#cite_note-51)

In the [classical era](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), as a result of the 3rd century BC [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") migrations, Bohemia became associated with the [Boii](/wiki/Boii "Boii").[[46]](#cite_note-Rankin2002-52) The Boii founded an [oppidum](/wiki/Oppidum "Oppidum") near the site of modern Prague.[[47]](#cite_note-(Firm)1997-53) Later in the 1st century, the Germanic tribes of the [Marcomanni](/wiki/Marcomanni "Marcomanni") and [Quadi](/wiki/Quadi "Quadi") settled there.[[48]](#cite_note-Salvia2007-54)

[Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") from the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea")–[Carpathian](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains") region settled in the area (their migration was pushed by an invasion of peoples from [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and Eastern Europe into their area:[[49]](#cite_note-Agnew2004-55) [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"), [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars"), [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars") and [Magyars](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians")).[[50]](#cite_note-HahnNadel2014-56) In the sixth century, the Huns had moved westwards into Bohemia, Moravia, and some of present-day Austria and Germany.[[50]](#cite_note-HahnNadel2014-56)

During the 7th century, the Frankish merchant [Samo](/wiki/Samo "Samo"), supporting the Slavs fighting against nearby settled [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars"),[[51]](#cite_note-BartlŠkvarna2002-57) became the ruler of the first documented Slavic state in Central Europe, [Samo's Empire](/wiki/Samo%27s_Empire "Samo's Empire").[[52]](#cite_note-58) The principality of [Great Moravia](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia"), controlled by [Moymir dynasty](/wiki/Moymirid_dynasty "Moymirid dynasty"), arose in the 8th century.[[53]](#cite_note-Champion2005-59) It reached its zenith in the 9th (during the reign of [Svatopluk I of Moravia](/wiki/Svatopluk_I_of_Moravia "Svatopluk I of Moravia")), holding off the influence of the Franks. Great Moravia was Christianized, with a role being played by the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") mission of [Cyril and Methodius](/wiki/Cyril_and_Methodius "Cyril and Methodius").[[54]](#cite_note-60) They codified the [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") language, the first literary and liturgical language of the [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"), and the [Glagolitic script](/wiki/Glagolitic_script "Glagolitic script").[[55]](#cite_note-BenedettoDuke2008-61)

### Bohemia

Main article: [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Locator_Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown_within_the_Holy_Roman_Empire_%281618%29.svg/250px-Locator_Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown_within_the_Holy_Roman_Empire_%281618%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Locator_Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown_within_the_Holy_Roman_Empire_(1618).svg)

The [Crown of Bohemia](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown "Lands of the Bohemian Crown") within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") (1600). The Czech lands were part of the Empire in 1002–1806, and Prague was the imperial seat in 1346–1437 and 1583–1611.

The principality of the [Bohemians](/wiki/Bohemians_(tribe) "Bohemians (tribe)") was represented by many dukes, and finally emerged as a centralized [Duchy of Bohemia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bohemia "Duchy of Bohemia") in the late 9th century when it was unified by the [Přemyslid dynasty](/wiki/P%C5%99emyslid_dynasty "Přemyslid dynasty"). Bohemia was from 1002 until 1806 an [Imperial Estate](/wiki/Imperial_Estate "Imperial Estate") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire").[[56]](#cite_note-PánekTůma2019-62)

In 1212, [Přemysl Ottokar I](/wiki/Ottokar_I_of_Bohemia "Ottokar I of Bohemia") extracted the [Golden Bull of Sicily](/wiki/Golden_Bull_of_Sicily "Golden Bull of Sicily") from the emperor, confirming Ottokar and his descendants' royal status; the [Duchy](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") of Bohemia was raised to a [Kingdom](/wiki/Realm "Realm").[[57]](#cite_note-PánekTůma2019b-63) [German immigrants](/wiki/Germans "Germans") settled in the Bohemian periphery in the 13th century.[[58]](#cite_note-PánekTůma2019c-64) The [Mongols](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") in the [invasion of Europe](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Europe "Mongol invasion of Europe") carried their raids into [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") but were defensively defeated at [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc").[[59]](#cite_note-65)

After a series of dynastic wars, the [House of Luxembourg](/wiki/House_of_Luxembourg "House of Luxembourg") gained the Bohemian throne.[[60]](#cite_note-66)

Efforts for a [reform of the church in Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemian_Reformation "Bohemian Reformation") started already in the late 14th century. [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus")' followers seceded from some practices of the [Roman Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and in the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars") (1419–1434) defeated five crusades organized against them by [Sigismund](/wiki/Sigismund,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Sigismund, Holy Roman Emperor"). During the next two centuries, 90% of the population in Bohemia and Moravia were considered [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites"). The pacifist thinker [Petr Chelčický](/wiki/Petr_Chel%C4%8Dick%C3%BD "Petr Chelčický") inspired the movement of the [Moravian Brethren](/wiki/Unity_of_the_Brethren_(Czech_Republic) "Unity of the Brethren (Czech Republic)") (by the middle of the 15th century) that completely separated from the Roman Catholic Church.[[61]](#cite_note-67)

[![Painting of battle between mounted knights](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Husit%C3%A9_-_Jensk%C3%BD_kodex.jpg/250px-Husit%C3%A9_-_Jensk%C3%BD_kodex.jpg)](/wiki/File:Husit%C3%A9_-_Jensk%C3%BD_kodex.jpg)

Battle between [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") and [crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") during the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"); Jena Codex, 15th century

On 21 December 1421, [Jan Žižka](/wiki/Jan_%C5%BDi%C5%BEka "Jan Žižka"), a successful military commander and mercenary, led his group of forces in the [Battle of Kutná Hora](/wiki/Battle_of_Kutn%C3%A1_Hora "Battle of Kutná Hora"), resulting in a victory for the Hussites. He is honoured to this day as a [national hero](/wiki/Hero_(title) "Hero (title)").

After 1526, Bohemia came increasingly under [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") control as the Habsburgs became first the elected and then in 1627 the hereditary rulers of Bohemia. Between 1583 and 1611 Prague was the official seat of the Holy Roman Emperor [Rudolf II](/wiki/Rudolf_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Rudolf II, Holy Roman Emperor") and his court.

The [Defenestration of Prague](/wiki/Defenestrations_of_Prague "Defenestrations of Prague") and subsequent revolt against the Habsburgs in 1618 marked the start of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"). In 1620, the rebellion in Bohemia was crushed at the [Battle of White Mountain](/wiki/Battle_of_White_Mountain "Battle of White Mountain") and the ties between Bohemia and the Habsburgs' hereditary lands in Austria were strengthened. The leaders of the [Bohemian Revolt](/wiki/Bohemian_Revolt "Bohemian Revolt") were [executed in 1621](/wiki/Old_Town_Square_execution "Old Town Square execution"). The nobility and the middle class Protestants had to either convert to Catholicism or leave the country.[[62]](#cite_note-68)

The following era of 1620 to the late 18th century became known as the "Dark Age". During the Thirty Years' War, the population of the [Czech lands](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands") declined by a third through the expulsion of Czech Protestants as well as due to the war, disease and [famine](/wiki/Famines_in_the_Czech_lands "Famines in the Czech lands").[[63]](#cite_note-69) The Habsburgs prohibited all Christian confessions other than [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[64]](#cite_note-70) The flowering of [Baroque culture](/wiki/Czech_Baroque_architecture "Czech Baroque architecture") shows the ambiguity of this historical period.
[Ottoman Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [Tatars](/wiki/Crimean_Khanate "Crimean Khanate") invaded Moravia in 1663.[[65]](#cite_note-71) In 1679–1680 the Czech lands faced the [Great Plague of Vienna](/wiki/Great_Plague_of_Vienna "Great Plague of Vienna") and an uprising of serfs.[[66]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Prager.Fenstersturz.1618.jpg/250px-Prager.Fenstersturz.1618.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prager.Fenstersturz.1618.jpg)

The 1618 [Defenestration of Prague](/wiki/Defenestrations_of_Prague "Defenestrations of Prague") marked the beginning of the [Bohemian Revolt](/wiki/Bohemian_Revolt "Bohemian Revolt") against the [Habsburgs](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") and therefore the first phase of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War").

There were peasant uprisings influenced by famine.[[67]](#cite_note-73) [Serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom") was abolished between 1781 and 1848. Several battles of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") took place on the current territory of the Czech Republic.

The end of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in 1806 led to degradation of the political status of Bohemia which lost its position of an [electorate](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") of the Holy Roman Empire as well as its own political representation in the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)").[[68]](#cite_note-74) Bohemian lands became part of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"). During the 18th and 19th century the [Czech National Revival](/wiki/Czech_National_Revival "Czech National Revival") began its rise, with the purpose to revive [Czech language](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), culture, and national identity. The [Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_Austrian_Empire "Revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire") in Prague, striving for liberal reforms and autonomy of the Bohemian Crown within the Austrian Empire, was suppressed.[[69]](#cite_note-auto-75)

It seemed that some concessions would be made also to Bohemia, but in the end, the Emperor [Franz Joseph I](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria") affected a compromise with Hungary only. The [Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Compromise_of_1867 "Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867") and the never realized coronation of Franz Joseph as King of Bohemia led to a disappointment of some Czech politicians.[[69]](#cite_note-auto-75) The Bohemian Crown lands became part of the so-called [Cisleithania](/wiki/Cisleithania "Cisleithania").

The Czech Social Democratic and progressive politicians started the fight for universal suffrage. The first elections under [universal male suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") were held in 1907.[[70]](#cite_note-76)

### Czechoslovakia

Main article: [History of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/History_of_Czechoslovakia "History of Czechoslovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Czechoslovakia_I.png/500px-Czechoslovakia_I.png)](/wiki/File:Czechoslovakia_I.png)

The [First Czechoslovak Republic](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic") comprised 27% of the population of the former Austria-Hungary and nearly 80% of the industry.[[71]](#cite_note-Stephen_J._Lee._Page_107.-77)

In 1918, during the collapse of the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") at the end of World War I, the independent republic of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), which joined the winning Allied powers, was created, with [Tomáš Garrigue Masaryk](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1%C5%A1_Garrigue_Masaryk "Tomáš Garrigue Masaryk") in the lead.[[72]](#cite_note-78) This new country incorporated the [Bohemian Crown](/wiki/Bohemian_Crown "Bohemian Crown").[[73]](#cite_note-79)

The [First Czechoslovak Republic](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic") comprised only 27% of the population of the former Austria-Hungary, but nearly 80% of the industry, which enabled it to compete with Western industrial states.[[71]](#cite_note-Stephen_J._Lee._Page_107.-77) In 1929 compared to 1913, the gross domestic product increased by 52% and industrial production by 41%. In 1938 Czechoslovakia held 10th place in the world industrial production.[[74]](#cite_note-80) Czechoslovakia was the only country in Central and Eastern Europe to remain a liberal democracy throughout the entire
interwar period.[[75]](#cite_note-81) Although the First Czechoslovak Republic was a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), it provided certain rights to its minorities, the largest being [Germans](/wiki/Germans_in_Czechoslovakia_(1918%E2%80%931938) "Germans in Czechoslovakia (1918–1938)") (23.6% in 1921), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians_in_Slovakia "Hungarians in Slovakia") (5.6%) and [Ukrainians](/wiki/Rusyns_and_Ukrainians_in_Czechoslovakia_(1918%E2%80%931938) "Rusyns and Ukrainians in Czechoslovakia (1918–1938)") (3.5%).[[76]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Pilots_of_No._310_%28Czechoslovak%29_Squadron_RAF_in_front_of_Hawker_Hurricane_Mk_I_at_Duxford%2C_Cambridgeshire%2C_7_September_1940._CH1299.jpg/250px-Pilots_of_No._310_%28Czechoslovak%29_Squadron_RAF_in_front_of_Hawker_Hurricane_Mk_I_at_Duxford%2C_Cambridgeshire%2C_7_September_1940._CH1299.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pilots_of_No._310_(Czechoslovak)_Squadron_RAF_in_front_of_Hawker_Hurricane_Mk_I_at_Duxford,_Cambridgeshire,_7_September_1940._CH1299.jpg)

Czechoslovak fighter pilots of [No. 310 Squadron RAF](/wiki/No._310_(Czechoslovak)_Squadron_RAF "No. 310 (Czechoslovak) Squadron RAF") during the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain") in 1940

Western Czechoslovakia was [occupied by Nazi Germany](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Czechoslovakia "German occupation of Czechoslovakia"), which placed most of the region into the [Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia"). The Protectorate was proclaimed part of the Third Reich, and the president and prime minister were subordinated to Nazi Germany's *[Reichsprotektor](/wiki/Protector_(title) "Protector (title)")*. [One Nazi concentration camp](/wiki/Theresienstadt_concentration_camp "Theresienstadt concentration camp") was located within the Czech territory at [Terezín](/wiki/Terez%C3%ADn "Terezín"), north of Prague. The vast majority of the Protectorate's Jews were murdered in [Nazi-run concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps"). The Nazi *[Generalplan Ost](/wiki/Generalplan_Ost "Generalplan Ost")* called for the extermination, expulsion, [Germanization](/wiki/Germanization "Germanization") or enslavement of most or all Czechs for the purpose of providing more [living space](/wiki/Lebensraum "Lebensraum") for the German people.[[77]](#cite_note-83) There was [Czechoslovak resistance to Nazi occupation](/wiki/Resistance_in_German-occupied_Czechoslovakia "Resistance in German-occupied Czechoslovakia") as well as [reprisals](/wiki/German_war_crimes "German war crimes") against the Czechoslovaks for their anti-Nazi resistance. The German occupation ended on 9 May 1945, with the arrival of the Soviet and American armies and the [Prague uprising](/wiki/Prague_uprising "Prague uprising").[[78]](#cite_note-84) Most of Czechoslovakia's German-speakers were forcibly [expelled](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Germans_from_Czechoslovakia "Expulsion of Germans from Czechoslovakia") from the country, first as a result of local acts of violence and then under the aegis of an "organized transfer" confirmed by the Soviet Union, the United States, and Great Britain at the [Potsdam Conference](/wiki/Potsdam_Conference "Potsdam Conference").[[79]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Praga_11.jpg/250px-Praga_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Praga_11.jpg)

Prague during the 1968 [Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact_invasion_of_Czechoslovakia "Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia")

In the [1946 elections](/wiki/Czechoslovakian_parliamentary_election,_1946 "Czechoslovakian parliamentary election, 1946"), the [Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Czechoslovakia "Communist Party of Czechoslovakia") gained 38%[[80]](#cite_note-86) of the votes and became the largest party in the Czechoslovak parliament, formed a coalition with other parties, and consolidated power. A [coup d'état](/wiki/1948_Czechoslovak_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1948 Czechoslovak coup d'état") came in 1948 and a single-party government was formed. For the [next 41 years](/wiki/History_of_Czechoslovakia_(1948%E2%80%931989) "History of Czechoslovakia (1948–1989)"), the Czechoslovak Communist state conformed to [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") economic and political features.[[81]](#cite_note-87) The [Prague Spring](/wiki/Prague_Spring "Prague Spring") political liberalization was stopped by the 1968 [Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact_invasion_of_Czechoslovakia "Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia"). Analysts believe that the invasion caused the communist movement to fracture, ultimately leading to the Revolutions of 1989.

### Czech Republic

Main articles: [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") and [Dissolution of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Czechoslovakia "Dissolution of Czechoslovakia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/V%C3%A1clav_Havel_cut_out.jpg/250px-V%C3%A1clav_Havel_cut_out.jpg)](/wiki/File:V%C3%A1clav_Havel_cut_out.jpg)

[Václav Havel](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Havel "Václav Havel"), one of the most important figures in Czech history during the 20th century—leader of the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution"), the last president of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") and the first president of the Czech Republic

In November 1989, Czechoslovakia again became a liberal democracy through the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution"). However, Slovak national aspirations strengthened ([Hyphen War](/wiki/Hyphen_War "Hyphen War")) and on 31 December 1992, the [country peacefully split](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Czechoslovakia "Dissolution of Czechoslovakia") into the independent [countries](/wiki/Country "Country") of the Czech Republic and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"). Both countries went through economic reforms and [privatizations](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization"), with the intention of creating a [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy"), as they have been trying to do since 1990, when Czechs and Slovaks still shared the common state. This process was largely successful; in 2006 the Czech Republic was recognized by the World Bank as a "developed country",[[82]](#cite_note-worldbank06-88) and in 2009 the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") ranked it as a nation of "Very High Human Development".[[83]](#cite_note-hdi09-89)

From 1991, the Czech Republic, originally as part of Czechoslovakia and since 1993 in its own right, has been a member of the [Visegrád Group](/wiki/Visegr%C3%A1d_Group "Visegrád Group") and from 1995, the [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"). The Czech Republic joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") on 12 March 1999 and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") on 1 May 2004. On 21 December 2007 the Czech Republic joined the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area").[[84]](#cite_note-90)

Until 2017, either the centre-left [Czech Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Czech_Social_Democratic_Party "Czech Social Democratic Party") or the centre-right [Civic Democratic Party](/wiki/Civic_Democratic_Party_(Czech_Republic) "Civic Democratic Party (Czech Republic)") led the governments of the Czech Republic. In October 2017, the populist movement [ANO 2011](/wiki/ANO_2011 "ANO 2011"), led by the country's second-richest man, [Andrej Babiš](/wiki/Andrej_Babi%C5%A1 "Andrej Babiš"), won the [elections](/wiki/2017_Czech_legislative_election "2017 Czech legislative election") with three times more votes than its closest rival, the Civic Democrats.[[85]](#cite_note-91) In December 2017, Czech president [Miloš Zeman](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1_Zeman "Miloš Zeman") appointed Andrej Babiš as the new prime minister.[[86]](#cite_note-92)

In the [2021 elections](/wiki/2021_Czech_legislative_election "2021 Czech legislative election"), [ANO 2011](/wiki/ANO_2011 "ANO 2011") was narrowly defeated and [Petr Fiala](/wiki/Petr_Fiala "Petr Fiala") became the new prime minister.[[87]](#cite_note-93) He formed a government coalition of the alliance [SPOLU](/wiki/Spolu_(Czech_Republic) "Spolu (Czech Republic)") ([Civic Democratic Party](/wiki/Civic_Democratic_Party_(Czech_Republic) "Civic Democratic Party (Czech Republic)"), [KDU-ČSL](/wiki/KDU-%C4%8CSL "KDU-ČSL") and [TOP 09](/wiki/TOP_09 "TOP 09")) and the alliance of [Pirates and Mayors](/wiki/Pirates_and_Mayors "Pirates and Mayors"). In January 2023, retired general [Petr Pavel](/wiki/Petr_Pavel "Petr Pavel") won the [presidential election](/wiki/2023_Czech_presidential_election "2023 Czech presidential election"), becoming new Czech president to succeed Miloš Zeman.[[88]](#cite_note-94)

On 24 February 2022, Russian forces began a large scale [war of aggression](/wiki/War_of_aggression "War of aggression") [against Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"). The Czech Republic took in half a million [Ukrainian refugees](/wiki/2022%E2%80%932023_Ukrainian_refugee_crisis "2022–2023 Ukrainian refugee crisis"), the largest number per capita in the world.[[89]](#cite_note-95)[[90]](#cite_note-96)

In December 2025, Babiš returned to office as the Czech Republic's prime minister,[[91]](#cite_note-97) following his populist ANO party's win in [October's parliamentary election](/wiki/2025_Czech_parliamentary_election "2025 Czech parliamentary election").[[92]](#cite_note-98)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Czech_Republic "Geography of the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/CzechRepublic-geographic_map-en.svg/250px-CzechRepublic-geographic_map-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CzechRepublic-geographic_map-en.svg)

Topographic map

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Krkono%C5%A1e_mountains_in_winter_2018_04.jpg/250px-Krkono%C5%A1e_mountains_in_winter_2018_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Krkono%C5%A1e_mountains_in_winter_2018_04.jpg)

[Giant Mountains](/wiki/Giant_Mountains "Giant Mountains") in winter

The Czech Republic lies mostly between latitudes [48°](/wiki/48th_parallel_north "48th parallel north") and [51° N](/wiki/51st_parallel_north "51st parallel north") and longitudes [12°](/wiki/12th_meridian_east "12th meridian east") and [19° E](/wiki/19th_meridian_east "19th meridian east").

[Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), to the west, consists of a basin drained by the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Labe*) and the [Vltava](/wiki/Vltava "Vltava") rivers, surrounded by mostly low mountains, such as the [Giant Mountains](/wiki/Giant_Mountains "Giant Mountains") range ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Krkonoše*) of the [Sudetes](/wiki/Sudetes "Sudetes"). The highest point in the country, [Sněžka](/wiki/Sn%C4%9B%C5%BEka "Sněžka") at 1,603 m (5,259 ft), is located here. Moravia, the eastern part of the country, is also hilly. It is drained mainly by the [Morava](/wiki/Morava_(river) "Morava (river)") River, but it also contains the source of the [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder") River ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Odra*).

Water from the Czech Republic flows to three different seas: the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"), and [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). The Czech Republic also leases the [Moldauhafen](/wiki/Moldauhafen "Moldauhafen"), a 30,000-square-meter (7.4-acre) [lot](/wiki/Lot_(real_estate) "Lot (real estate)") in the middle of the [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") Docks, which was awarded to Czechoslovakia by Article 363 of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), to allow the landlocked country a place where goods transported down river could be transferred to seagoing ships. The territory reverts to Germany in 2028.

[Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), the Czech Republic belongs to the Central European province of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region"), within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom"). According to the [World Wide Fund for Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature"), the territory of the Czech Republic can be subdivided into four [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"): the [Western European broadleaf forests](/wiki/Western_European_broadleaf_forests "Western European broadleaf forests"), [Central European mixed forests](/wiki/Central_European_mixed_forests "Central European mixed forests"), [Pannonian mixed forests](/wiki/Pannonian_mixed_forests "Pannonian mixed forests"), and [Carpathian montane conifer forests](/wiki/Carpathian_montane_conifer_forests "Carpathian montane conifer forests").[[93]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-99)

There are four [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") in the Czech Republic. The oldest is [Krkonoše National Park](/wiki/Krkono%C5%A1e_National_Park "Krkonoše National Park") ([Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Man_and_the_Biosphere_Programme "Man and the Biosphere Programme")), and the others are [Šumava National Park](/wiki/%C5%A0umava_National_Park "Šumava National Park") (Biosphere Reserve), [Podyjí National Park](/wiki/Podyj%C3%AD_National_Park "Podyjí National Park"), and [Bohemian Switzerland](/wiki/Bohemian_Switzerland "Bohemian Switzerland").

The three historical lands of the Czech Republic (formerly some countries of the Bohemian Crown) correspond with the river basins of the Elbe and the Vltava basin for Bohemia, the Morava one for Moravia, and the Oder river basin for Czech Silesia (in terms of the Czech territory).

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Czech_Republic_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Czech_Republic_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Czech_Republic_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

Köppen climate classification types of the Czech Republic using the −3 °C isotherm:

[Humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate")

[Oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate")

[Subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate")

The Czech Republic has a temperate climate, situated in the transition zone between the [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") and [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") types, with warm summers and cold, cloudy and snowy winters. The temperature difference between summer and winter is due to the landlocked geographical position.[[94]](#cite_note-100)

Temperatures vary depending on the elevation. In general, at higher altitudes, the temperatures decrease and [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") increases. The wettest area in the Czech Republic is found around [Bílý Potok](/wiki/B%C3%ADl%C3%BD_Potok_(Liberec_District) "Bílý Potok (Liberec District)") in [Jizera Mountains](/wiki/Jizera_Mountains "Jizera Mountains") and the driest region is the [Louny District](/wiki/Louny_District "Louny District") to the northwest of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). Another factor is the distribution of the mountains.

At the highest peak of [Sněžka](/wiki/Sn%C4%9B%C5%BEka "Sněžka") (1,603 m or 5,259 ft), the average temperature is −0.4 °C (31 °F), whereas in the lowlands of the [South Moravian Region](/wiki/South_Moravian_Region "South Moravian Region"), the average temperature is as high as 10 °C (50 °F). The country's capital, Prague, has a similar average temperature, although this is influenced by urban factors.

The coldest month is usually January, followed by February and December. During these months, there is [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") in the mountains and sometimes in the cities and lowlands. During March, April, and May, the temperature usually increases, especially during April, when the temperature and weather tends to vary during the day. Spring is also characterized by higher water levels in the rivers, due to melting snow with occasional flooding.

The warmest month of the year is July, followed by August and June. On average, summer temperatures are about 20–30 °C (36–54 °F) higher than during winter. Summer is also characterized by rain and storms.

Autumn generally begins in September, which is still warm and dry. During October, temperatures usually fall below 15 °C (59 °F) or 10 °C (50 °F) and [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees begin to shed their leaves. By the end of November, temperatures usually range around the freezing point.

The coldest temperature ever measured was −42.2 °C (−44.0 °F) in [Litvínovice](/wiki/Litv%C3%ADnovice "Litvínovice") near [České Budějovice](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_Bud%C4%9Bjovice "České Budějovice") in 1929, and the hottest measured was 40.4 °C (104.7 °F) in [Dobřichovice](/wiki/Dob%C5%99ichovice "Dobřichovice") in 2012.[[95]](#cite_note-101)

Most [rain](/wiki/Rain "Rain") falls during the summer. Sporadic rainfall is throughout the year (in Prague, the average number of days per month experiencing at least 0.1 mm (0.0039 in) of rain varies from 12 in September and October to 16 in November) but concentrated rainfall (days with more than 10 mm (0.39 in) per day) are more frequent in the months of May to August (average around two such days per month).[[96]](#cite_note-102) Severe [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm"), producing damaging straight-line winds, [hail](/wiki/Hail "Hail"), and occasional [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") occur, especially during the summer period.[[97]](#cite_note-103)[[98]](#cite_note-104)

### Biodiversity and conservation

See also: [Protected areas of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_the_Czech_Republic "Protected areas of the Czech Republic") and [Fauna of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Fauna_of_the_Czech_Republic "Fauna of the Czech Republic")

As of 2020, the Czech Republic ranks as the 21st most environmentally conscious country in the world in [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index").[[99]](#cite_note-105) It had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 1.71/10, ranking it 160th globally out of 172 countries.[[100]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-106) The Czech Republic has four National Parks ([Šumava National Park](/wiki/%C5%A0umava_National_Park "Šumava National Park"), [Krkonoše National Park](/wiki/Krkono%C5%A1e_National_Park "Krkonoše National Park"), [České Švýcarsko National Park](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_%C5%A0v%C3%BDcarsko_National_Park "České Švýcarsko National Park"), [Podyjí National Park](/wiki/Podyj%C3%AD_National_Park "Podyjí National Park")) and 25 Protected Landscape Areas. The fauna of the Czech Republic includes a wide variety of [animal](/wiki/Animal "Animal") [species](/wiki/Species "Species"). Some species (especially [endangered](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species") ones) are bred in [reserves](/wiki/Game_reserve "Game reserve"). Among the rare animals are, for example, eagles, ospreys, bustards, and storks.[[101]](#cite_note-107)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Government_of_the_Czech_Republic "Government of the Czech Republic") and [Politics of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Czech_Republic "Politics of the Czech Republic")

[![Petr Pavel](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Petr_Pavel.jpg/250px-Petr_Pavel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petr_Pavel.jpg)

[Petr Pavel](/wiki/Petr_Pavel "Petr Pavel")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_the_Czech_Republic "President of the Czech Republic")

[![Andrej Babiš](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Andrej_Babi%C5%A1_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Andrej_Babi%C5%A1_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrej_Babi%C5%A1_2025_(cropped).jpg)

[Andrej Babiš](/wiki/Andrej_Babi%C5%A1 "Andrej Babiš")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Czech_Republic "Prime Minister of the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Slib_poslanc%C5%AF_8._listopadu_2021.jpg/250px-Slib_poslanc%C5%AF_8._listopadu_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slib_poslanc%C5%AF_8._listopadu_2021.jpg)

The [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chamber of Deputies of the Czech Republic"), lower house of the [Parliament of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Czech_Republic "Parliament of the Czech Republic")

The Czech Republic is a pluralist multi-party [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"). The [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Czech_Republic "Parliament of the Czech Republic") (*Parlament České republiky*) is bicameral, with the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chamber of Deputies of the Czech Republic") ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Poslanecká sněmovna*, 200 members) and the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Czech_Republic "Senate of the Czech Republic") ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *Senát*, 81 members).[[102]](#cite_note-108) The [members of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament#Czech_Republic "Member of Parliament") are elected for a four-year term by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), with a 5% [election threshold](/wiki/Election_threshold "Election threshold"). There are 14 voting districts, identical to the country's administrative regions. The Chamber of Deputies, the successor to the [Czech National Council](/wiki/Czech_National_Council "Czech National Council"), has the powers and responsibilities of the now defunct federal parliament of the former Czechoslovakia. The members of the Senate are elected in single-seat [constituencies](/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district") by two-round [runoff voting](/wiki/Two-round_system "Two-round system") for a six-year term, with one-third elected every even year in the autumn. This arrangement is modeled on the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), but each constituency is roughly the same size and the voting system used is a two-round runoff.

The president is a formal head of state with limited and specific powers, who appoints the prime minister, as well the other members of the cabinet on a proposal by the prime minister. From 1993 until 2012, the [President of the Czech Republic](/wiki/President_of_the_Czech_Republic "President of the Czech Republic") was selected by a joint session of the parliament for a five-year term, with no more than two consecutive terms ([Václav Havel](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Havel "Václav Havel") and [Václav Klaus](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Klaus "Václav Klaus") were both elected twice). Since 2013, the president has been elected directly.[[103]](#cite_note-109) Some commentators have argued that, with the introduction of direct election of the President, the Czech Republic has moved away from the parliamentary system and towards a [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") one.[[104]](#cite_note-110) The [Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Czech_Republic "Government of the Czech Republic")'s exercise of executive power derives from the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitution of the Czech Republic"). The members of the government are the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Czech_Republic "Prime Minister of the Czech Republic"), Deputy prime ministers and other ministers. The Government is responsible to the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chamber of Deputies of the Czech Republic").[[105]](#cite_note-111) The Prime Minister is the head of government and wields powers such as the right to set the agenda for most foreign and domestic policy and choose government ministers.[[106]](#cite_note-112)

### Law

Main articles: [Law of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Law_of_the_Czech_Republic "Law of the Czech Republic"), [Judiciary of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Judiciary_of_the_Czech_Republic "Judiciary of the Czech Republic"), and [Law enforcement in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_the_Czech_Republic "Law enforcement in the Czech Republic")

See also: [Gun law in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Gun_law_in_the_Czech_Republic "Gun law in the Czech Republic") and [Self-defence (Czech Republic)](/wiki/Self-defence_(Czech_Republic) "Self-defence (Czech Republic)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/%C3%9Astavn%C3%AD_soud%2C_Brno_Jo%C5%A1tova_4277_sn%C4%9Bmovna.jpg/250px-%C3%9Astavn%C3%AD_soud%2C_Brno_Jo%C5%A1tova_4277_sn%C4%9Bmovna.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%9Astavn%C3%AD_soud,_Brno_Jo%C5%A1tova_4277_sn%C4%9Bmovna.jpg)

Interior of the [Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic") in [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno")

The Czech Republic is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"),[[107]](#cite_note-CabadaWaisová2011-113) with a [civil law system](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") based on the continental type, rooted in Germanic legal culture. The basis of the legal system is the [Constitution of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitution of the Czech Republic") adopted in 1993.[[108]](#cite_note-Kuklík2015-114) The [Penal Code](/wiki/Criminal_code "Criminal code") is effective from 2010. A new [Civil code](/wiki/Civil_code "Civil code") became effective in 2014. The court system includes district, county, and supreme courts and is divided into civil, criminal, and administrative branches. The Czech judiciary has a [triumvirate](/wiki/Triumvirate "Triumvirate") of supreme courts. The [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic") consists of 15 constitutional judges and oversees violations of the Constitution by either the legislature or by the [government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Czech_Republic "Government of the Czech Republic").[[108]](#cite_note-Kuklík2015-114) The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Supreme Court of the Czech Republic") is formed of 67 judges and is the court of highest appeal for most [legal cases](/wiki/Legal_case "Legal case") heard in the Czech Republic. The [Supreme Administrative Court](/wiki/Supreme_Administrative_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Supreme Administrative Court of the Czech Republic") decides on issues of procedural and administrative propriety. It also has jurisdiction over certain political matters, such as the formation and closure of political parties, jurisdictional boundaries between government entities, and the eligibility of persons to stand for public office.[[108]](#cite_note-Kuklík2015-114) The Supreme Court and the Supreme Administrative Court are both based in [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"), as is the Supreme Public Prosecutor's Office.[[108]](#cite_note-Kuklík2015-114)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_Czech_Republic "Foreign relations of the Czech Republic")

See also: [Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_of_the_Czech_Republic "Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Visa_requirements_for_Czech_citizens.svg/250px-Visa_requirements_for_Czech_citizens.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Visa_requirements_for_Czech_citizens.svg)

Visa-free entry countries for Czech citizens in green, EU in blue (see [citizenship of the European Union](/wiki/Citizenship_of_the_European_Union "Citizenship of the European Union"))

The Czech Republic has ranked as [one of the safest or most peaceful countries](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index") for the past few decades.[[109]](#cite_note-115) It is a member of the United Nations, the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") and is an observer to the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States").[[110]](#cite_note-116) The embassies of most countries with diplomatic relations with the Czech Republic are located in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), while [consulates](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") are located across the country.

The Czech passport is [restricted by visas](/wiki/Visa_requirements_for_Czech_citizens "Visa requirements for Czech citizens"). According to the 2018 [Henley & Partners Visa Restrictions Index](/wiki/Henley_%26_Partners_Visa_Restrictions_Index "Henley & Partners Visa Restrictions Index"), Czech citizens have visa-free access to 173 countries, which ranks them 7th along with [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").[[111]](#cite_note-117) The [World Tourism Organization](/wiki/World_Tourism_Organization "World Tourism Organization") ranks the Czech passport 24th.[[112]](#cite_note-118) The US [Visa Waiver Program](/wiki/Visa_Waiver_Program "Visa Waiver Program") applies to Czech nationals.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Czernin_Palast_Prag.jpg/250px-Czernin_Palast_Prag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Czernin_Palast_Prag.jpg)

[Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Czech_Republic) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Czech Republic)") in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague")

The [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Czech_Republic "Prime Minister of the Czech Republic") and [Minister of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Minister_of_Foreign_Affairs_of_the_Czech_Republic "Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Czech Republic") have primary roles in setting foreign policy, although the [President](/wiki/President_of_the_Czech_Republic "President of the Czech Republic") also has influence and represents the country abroad. Membership in the European Union and NATO is central to the Czech Republic's foreign policy. The [Office for Foreign Relations and Information](/wiki/Office_for_Foreign_Relations_and_Information "Office for Foreign Relations and Information") (ÚZSI) serves as the [foreign intelligence](/wiki/Foreign_intelligence "Foreign intelligence") agency responsible for [espionage](/wiki/Espionage "Espionage") and foreign policy briefings, as well as protection of Czech Republic's embassies abroad.

The Czech Republic has ties with [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") as a member of the [Visegrád Group](/wiki/Visegr%C3%A1d_Group "Visegrád Group"),[[113]](#cite_note-119) as well as with Germany,[[114]](#cite_note-120) [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"),[[115]](#cite_note-121) the United States[[116]](#cite_note-122) and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and its [members](/wiki/Members_of_the_European_Union "Members of the European Union"). After 2020, relations with Asian democratic states, such as [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), are being strengthened.[[117]](#cite_note-123) Conversely, the Czech Republic has long had bad relations with [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"); from 2021, the Czech Republic appears on Russia's official list of enemy countries.[[118]](#cite_note-124) The Czech Republic also has problematic relations with [China](/wiki/China "China").

Czech officials have supported dissenters in [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") and [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba").[[119]](#cite_note-democrats-125)

Famous Czech diplomats of the past included [Jaroslav Lev of Rožmitál](/wiki/Jaroslav_Lev_of_Ro%C5%BEmit%C3%A1l "Jaroslav Lev of Rožmitál"), [Humprecht Jan Czernin](/wiki/Humprecht_Jan_Czernin "Humprecht Jan Czernin"), Count [Philip Kinsky of Wchinitz and Tettau](/wiki/Philip_Kinsky_of_Wchinitz_and_Tettau "Philip Kinsky of Wchinitz and Tettau"), [Wenzel Anton, Prince of Kaunitz-Rietberg](/wiki/Wenzel_Anton,_Prince_of_Kaunitz-Rietberg "Wenzel Anton, Prince of Kaunitz-Rietberg"), Prince [Karl Philipp Schwarzenberg](/wiki/Karl_Philipp,_Prince_of_Schwarzenberg "Karl Philipp, Prince of Schwarzenberg"), [Alois Lexa von Aehrenthal](/wiki/Alois_Lexa_von_Aehrenthal "Alois Lexa von Aehrenthal"), [Ottokar Czernin](/wiki/Ottokar_Czernin "Ottokar Czernin"), [Edvard Beneš](/wiki/Edvard_Bene%C5%A1 "Edvard Beneš"), [Jan Masaryk](/wiki/Jan_Masaryk "Jan Masaryk"), [Jiří Hájek](/wiki/Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_H%C3%A1jek "Jiří Hájek"), [Jiří Dienstbier](/wiki/Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Dienstbier "Jiří Dienstbier"), [Michael Žantovský](/wiki/Michael_%C5%BDantovsk%C3%BD "Michael Žantovský"), [Petr Kolář](/wiki/Petr_Kol%C3%A1%C5%99 "Petr Kolář"), [Alexandr Vondra](/wiki/Alexandr_Vondra "Alexandr Vondra"), Prince [Karel Schwarzenberg](/wiki/Karel_Schwarzenberg "Karel Schwarzenberg") and [Petr Pavel](/wiki/Petr_Pavel "Petr Pavel").

### Military

Main article: [Army of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Army_of_the_Czech_Republic "Army of the Czech Republic")

See also: [Designated Reserves (Czech Republic)](/wiki/Designated_Reserves_(Czech_Republic) "Designated Reserves (Czech Republic)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Dejvice%2C_gener%C3%A1ln%C3%AD_%C5%A1t%C3%A1b.jpg/250px-Dejvice%2C_gener%C3%A1ln%C3%AD_%C5%A1t%C3%A1b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dejvice,_gener%C3%A1ln%C3%AD_%C5%A1t%C3%A1b.jpg)

General Staff of the [Army of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Army_of_the_Czech_Republic "Army of the Czech Republic") in Prague

The [Czech armed forces](/wiki/Army_of_the_Czech_Republic "Army of the Czech Republic") consist of the [Czech Land Forces](/wiki/Czech_Land_Forces "Czech Land Forces"), the [Czech Air Force](/wiki/Czech_Air_Force "Czech Air Force") and of specialized support units. The armed forces are managed by the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Czech_Republic) "Ministry of Defence (Czech Republic)"). The [President of the Czech Republic](/wiki/President_of_the_Czech_Republic "President of the Czech Republic") is [Commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the armed forces. In 2004 the army transformed itself into a fully professional organization and compulsory military service was abolished. The country has been a member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") since 12 March 1999. Defence spending is approximately 1.28% of the GDP (2021).[[120]](#cite_note-126) The armed forces are charged with protecting the Czech Republic and its allies, promoting global security interests, and contributing to NATO.

Currently, as a member of NATO, the Czech military are participating in the [Resolute Support](/wiki/Resolute_Support_Mission "Resolute Support Mission") and [KFOR](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force") operations and have soldiers in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and [Somalia](/wiki/Operation_Atalanta "Operation Atalanta"). The Czech Air Force also served in the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") and [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland").[[121]](#cite_note-127) The main equipment of the Czech military includes [JAS 39 Gripen](/wiki/Saab_JAS_39_Gripen "Saab JAS 39 Gripen") multi-role fighters, [Aero L-159 Alca](/wiki/Aero_L-159_Alca "Aero L-159 Alca") combat aircraft, [AH-1Z Viper](/wiki/Bell_AH-1Z_Viper "Bell AH-1Z Viper") attack helicopters, armored vehicles ([Pandur II](/wiki/Pandur_II "Pandur II"), [BVP-2](/wiki/BMP-2 "BMP-2")) and tanks ([T-72M4CZ](/wiki/T-72M4CZ "T-72M4CZ") and [Leopard 2A4](/wiki/Leopard_2A4 "Leopard 2A4")).

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Czech_Republic "Human rights in the Czech Republic")

See also: [LGBT rights in the Czech Republic](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_Czech_Republic "LGBT rights in the Czech Republic")

Human rights in the Czech Republic are guaranteed by the [Charter of Fundamental Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_and_Freedoms "Charter of Fundamental Rights and Freedoms") and [international treaties](/wiki/International_treaties "International treaties") on human rights. Nevertheless, there were cases of human rights violations such as discrimination against Roma children,[[122]](#cite_note-128) for which the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") asked the Czech Republic to provide an explanation,[[123]](#cite_note-129) or the illegal sterilization of Roma women,[[124]](#cite_note-130) for which the government apologized.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

People of the same sex can enter into a "[registered partnership](/wiki/Registered_partnership "Registered partnership")" in the Czech Republic. Conducting [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") is not legal under current Czech law.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Regions of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic "Regions of the Czech Republic"), [Districts of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Districts_of_the_Czech_Republic "Districts of the Czech Republic"), and [Obec](/wiki/Obec "Obec")

Since 2000, the Czech Republic has been divided into [thirteen regions](/wiki/Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic "Regions of the Czech Republic") ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): [*kraje*, singular *kraj*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/en:kraj#Czech "wikt:en:kraj")) and the capital city of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). Every region has its own elected regional assembly and a regional governor. In Prague, the assembly and presidential powers are executed by the city council and the mayor.

The older seventy-six [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_the_Czech_Republic "Districts of the Czech Republic") (*okresy*, singular *[okres](/wiki/Okres "Okres")*) including three "statutory cities" (without Prague, which had special status) lost most of their importance in 1999 in an administrative reform; they remain as territorial divisions and seats of various branches of state administration.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

The smallest administrative units are [*obce* (municipalities)](/wiki/Obec "Obec"). As of 2021, the Czech Republic is divided into 6,254 municipalities. Cities and towns are also municipalities. The capital city of Prague is a region and municipality at the same time.
