## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Czech_Republic "Economy of the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Czechia.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Czechia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Czechia.svg)

Real GDP per capita in the Czech Republic from 1973 to 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Schengenzone.svg/250px-Schengenzone.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Schengenzone.svg)

The Czech Republic is part of the [European Single Market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market") and the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") (dark and light blue on the map above), but uses its own currency, the [Czech koruna](/wiki/Czech_koruna "Czech koruna").

The Czech Republic has a [developed](/wiki/IMF_advanced_economy_list "IMF advanced economy list"),[[128]](#cite_note-134) [high-income](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy")[[129]](#cite_note-135) [export-oriented](/wiki/Trading_nation "Trading nation") [social market economy](/wiki/Social_market_economy "Social market economy") based in services, manufacturing and innovation, that maintains a [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") and the [European social model](/wiki/European_social_model "European social model").[[130]](#cite_note-An_Ideal-Typical_Perspective-136) The Czech Republic participates in the [European Single Market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market") as a member of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and is therefore a part of the [economy of the European Union](/wiki/Economy_of_the_European_Union "Economy of the European Union"), but uses its own currency, the [Czech koruna](/wiki/Czech_koruna "Czech koruna"), instead of the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"). It has a per capita GDP rate that is 91% of the EU average[[131]](#cite_note-137) and is a member of the [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"). [Monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") is conducted by the [Czech National Bank](/wiki/Czech_National_Bank "Czech National Bank"), whose independence is guaranteed by the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitution of the Czech Republic"). The Czech Republic ranks 12th in the [UN](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") [inequality-adjusted human development](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_inequality-adjusted_HDI "List of countries by inequality-adjusted HDI") and 24th in [World Bank Human Capital Index](/wiki/World_Bank_Human_Capital_Index "World Bank Human Capital Index"). It was described by *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* as "one of Europe's most flourishing economies".[[132]](#cite_note-138)

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit), the country's [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") at [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") is $51,329[[133]](#cite_note-139) and $29,856 at [nominal value](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)").[[134]](#cite_note-IMFWEOCZ201901-140) According to Allianz A.G., in 2024 the country was an MWC (mean wealth country), ranking 24th in net financial assets.[[135]](#cite_note-141) The country experienced a 4.5% [GDP growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") in 2017.[[136]](#cite_note-142) The 2016 [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") rate was the lowest in the EU at 2.4%,[[137]](#cite_note-Eurostat_Unemployment_2016-143) and the 2016 poverty rate was the second lowest of OECD members.[[138]](#cite_note-FT_19_October_2016-144) 32nd in the 2025 [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index"),[[139]](#cite_note-145)[[140]](#cite_note-146) 29th in the [Global Competitiveness Report](/wiki/Global_Competitiveness_Report "Global Competitiveness Report"),[[141]](#cite_note-GCR_2018/19-147) and 25th in the [Global Enabling Trade Report](/wiki/Global_Enabling_Trade_Report "Global Enabling Trade Report").[[142]](#cite_note-148) The Czech Republic has a diverse economy that [ranks 7th](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_economic_complexity "List of countries by economic complexity") in the 2016 [Economic Complexity Index](/wiki/Economic_Complexity_Index "Economic Complexity Index").[[143]](#cite_note-Atlas_of_Economic_Complexity-149) The industrial sector accounts for 37.5% of the economy, while services account for 60% and agriculture for 2.5%.[[144]](#cite_note-150) The largest trading partner for both export and import is [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and the EU in general. Dividends worth CZK 270 billion were paid to the [foreign owners](/wiki/Foreign_ownership "Foreign ownership") of Czech companies in 2017, which has become a political issue.[[145]](#cite_note-151) The country has been a member of the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") since 1 May 2004, having abolished border controls, completely opening its borders with all of its neighbors on 21 December 2007.[[146]](#cite_note-152)

### Industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Skoda_Octavia_IV_RS_IMG_4250.jpg/250px-Skoda_Octavia_IV_RS_IMG_4250.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skoda_Octavia_IV_RS_IMG_4250.jpg)

[Škoda Octavia](/wiki/%C5%A0koda_Octavia "Škoda Octavia") RS iV

In 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit) the [largest companies](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_the_Czech_Republic#Largest_by_revenue "List of companies of the Czech Republic") by revenue in the Czech Republic were: automobile manufacturer [Škoda Auto](/wiki/%C5%A0koda_Auto "Škoda Auto"), utility company [ČEZ Group](/wiki/%C4%8CEZ_Group "ČEZ Group"), conglomerate [Agrofert](/wiki/Agrofert "Agrofert"), energy trading company [EPH](/wiki/Energetick%C3%BD_a_pr%C5%AFmyslov%C3%BD_holding "Energetický a průmyslový holding"), oil processing company [Unipetrol](/wiki/Unipetrol "Unipetrol"), electronics manufacturer [Foxconn CZ](/wiki/Foxconn_CZ "Foxconn CZ") and steel producer [Moravia Steel](/wiki/T%C5%99inec_Iron_and_Steel_Works "Třinec Iron and Steel Works").[[147]](#cite_note-153) Other Czech transportation companies include: [Škoda Transportation](/wiki/%C5%A0koda_Transportation "Škoda Transportation") (tramways, trolleybuses, metro), [Tatra](/wiki/Tatra_(company) "Tatra (company)") (heavy trucks, the second oldest car maker in the world), [Avia](/wiki/Avia_Motors "Avia Motors") (medium trucks), [Karosa](/wiki/Karosa "Karosa") and [SOR Libchavy](/wiki/SOR_Libchavy "SOR Libchavy") (buses), [Aero Vodochody](/wiki/Aero_Vodochody "Aero Vodochody") (military aircraft), [Let Kunovice](/wiki/Let_Kunovice "Let Kunovice") (civil aircraft), [Zetor](/wiki/Zetor "Zetor") (tractors), [Jawa Moto](/wiki/Jawa_Moto "Jawa Moto") (motorcycles) and [Čezeta](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A1_zbrojovka_Strakonice "Česká zbrojovka Strakonice") (electric scooters).

Škoda Transportation is the fourth largest [tram](/wiki/Tram "Tram") producer in the world; nearly one third of all trams in the world come from Czech factories.[[148]](#cite_note-154) The Czech Republic is also the world's largest [vinyl records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") manufacturer, with [GZ Media](/wiki/GZ_Media "GZ Media") producing about 6 million pieces annually in [Loděnice](/wiki/Lod%C4%9Bnice_(Beroun_District) "Loděnice (Beroun District)").[[149]](#cite_note-155)

Defense has also been a traditionally important part of industry, with Czechoslovakia having been the world's largest exporter of arms in 1934 and 1935.[[150]](#cite_note-Euro_platinum-156) In 2025, the largest defense companies included the [Czechoslovak Group](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Group "Czechoslovak Group") (worth € 31,3 billion), [STV Group](/w/index.php?title=STV_Group_(Arms_Company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "STV Group (Arms Company) (page does not exist)") (worth € 1,13 billion), [Colt CZ Group](/wiki/Colt_CZ_Group "Colt CZ Group") (worth € 1,09 billion) and [Omnipol](/wiki/Omnipol "Omnipol") (worth € 0,58 billion).[[150]](#cite_note-Euro_platinum-156)

In the [food industry](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry"), Czech companies include [Agrofert](/wiki/Agrofert "Agrofert"), [Kofola](/wiki/Kofola "Kofola") and [Hamé](/w/index.php?title=Ham%C3%A9_(company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hamé (company) (page does not exist)").

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Energy_in_the_Czech_Republic "Energy in the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Nuclear.power.plant.Dukovany.jpg/250px-Nuclear.power.plant.Dukovany.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nuclear.power.plant.Dukovany.jpg)

[Dukovany Nuclear Power Station](/wiki/Dukovany_Nuclear_Power_Station "Dukovany Nuclear Power Station")

Production of Czech electricity exceeds consumption by about 10 [TWh](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour "Kilowatt hour") per year, the excess being exported. [Nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") presently provides about 30 percent of the total power needs, its share is projected to increase to 40 percent. In 2005, 65.4 percent of electricity was produced by steam and combustion power plants (mostly coal); 30 percent by nuclear plants; and 4.6 percent came from renewable sources, including hydropower. The largest Czech power resource is [Temelín Nuclear Power Station](/wiki/Temel%C3%ADn_Nuclear_Power_Station "Temelín Nuclear Power Station"), with another nuclear power plant in [Dukovany](/wiki/Dukovany_Nuclear_Power_Station "Dukovany Nuclear Power Station").

The Czech Republic is reducing its dependence on highly polluting low-grade [brown coal](/wiki/Lignite "Lignite") as a source of energy. Natural gas is purchased from [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway "Norway") companies and as liquefied gas LNG from the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). In the past, three-quarters of gas supplies came from [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), but after the start of the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the government gradually stopped these supplies.[[151]](#cite_note-157) Gas consumption (approx. 100 TWh in 2003–2005) is almost double electricity consumption. South Moravia has small [oil and gas deposits](/wiki/Oil_and_gas_deposits_in_the_Czech_Republic "Oil and gas deposits in the Czech Republic").

### Transport infrastructure

Main article: [Transport in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Transport_in_the_Czech_Republic "Transport in the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Prague_V%C3%A1clav_Havel_Airport_in_2019.02.jpg/250px-Prague_V%C3%A1clav_Havel_Airport_in_2019.02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prague_V%C3%A1clav_Havel_Airport_in_2019.02.jpg)

[Václav Havel Airport Prague](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Havel_Airport_Prague "Václav Havel Airport Prague")

As of 2020,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit) the road network in the Czech Republic is 55,768.3 kilometers (34,652.82 mi) long, out of which 1,276.4 km (793.1 mi) are motorways.[[152]](#cite_note-158) The [speed limit](/wiki/Speed_limits_by_country "Speed limits by country") is 50 km/h (31 mph) within towns, 90 km/h (56 mph) outside of towns and 130 km/h (81 mph) on motorways.[[153]](#cite_note-159)

The Czech Republic has one of the [densest rail networks in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_rail_transport_network_size "List of countries by rail transport network size"). As of 2020,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit) the country has 9,542 kilometers (5,929 mi) of lines. Of that number, 3,236 km (2,011 mi) is electrified, 7,503 km (4,662 mi) are single-line tracks and 2,040 km (1,270 mi) are double and multiple-line tracks.[[154]](#cite_note-160) The length of tracks is 15,360 km (9,540 mi), out of which 6,917 km (4,298 mi) is electrified.[[155]](#cite_note-161)

[České dráhy](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_dr%C3%A1hy "České dráhy") (lit. 'Czech railways') is the main railway operator in the country, with about 170 million passengers carried annually.[[156]](#cite_note-162) Maximum speed is limited to 160 km/h (99 mph).

[Václav Havel Airport Prague](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Havel_Airport_Prague "Václav Havel Airport Prague") is the main international airport in the country. In 2019, it handled 17.8 million passengers.[[157]](#cite_note-163) In total, the Czech Republic has [91 airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of airports in the Czech Republic"), six of which provide international air services. The public international airports are in [Brno](/wiki/Brno-Tu%C5%99any_Airport "Brno-Tuřany Airport"), [Karlovy Vary](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary_Airport "Karlovy Vary Airport"), [Mnichovo Hradiště](/w/index.php?title=Mnichovo_Hradi%C5%A1t%C4%9B_Airport&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mnichovo Hradiště Airport (page does not exist)"), [Mošnov](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Airport_Ostrava "Leoš Janáček Airport Ostrava") (near [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava")), [Pardubice](/wiki/Pardubice_Airport "Pardubice Airport") and Prague.[[158]](#cite_note-164) The non-public international airports capable of handling airliners are in [Kunovice](/wiki/Kunovice_Airport "Kunovice Airport") and [Vodochody](/wiki/Vodochody_Airport "Vodochody Airport").[[159]](#cite_note-165)

Russia (via pipelines through Ukraine) and, to a lesser extent, Norway (via pipelines through Germany) supply the Czech Republic with liquid and natural gas.[[160]](#cite_note-guidepost-166)

### Communications and IT

Main article: [Internet in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Internet_in_the_Czech_Republic "Internet in the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Eduard_Ku%C4%8Dera%2C_Milada_Ku%C4%8Derov%C3%A1%2C_Jarmila_Baudi%C5%A1ov%C3%A1%2C_Pavel_Baudi%C5%A1%2C_foto_Milan_Bure%C5%A1.jpg/250px-Eduard_Ku%C4%8Dera%2C_Milada_Ku%C4%8Derov%C3%A1%2C_Jarmila_Baudi%C5%A1ov%C3%A1%2C_Pavel_Baudi%C5%A1%2C_foto_Milan_Bure%C5%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eduard_Ku%C4%8Dera,_Milada_Ku%C4%8Derov%C3%A1,_Jarmila_Baudi%C5%A1ov%C3%A1,_Pavel_Baudi%C5%A1,_foto_Milan_Bure%C5%A1.jpg)

Founders and owners of the antivirus group [Avast](/wiki/Avast "Avast")

On 1 July 2006, Český Telecom was acquired by globalized company (Spain-owned) Telefónica group and adopted the new name [Telefónica O2 Czech Republic](/wiki/Telef%C3%B3nica_O2_Czech_Republic "Telefónica O2 Czech Republic"). As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit), VDSL and ADSL2+ are offered in variants, with download speeds of up to 50 Mbit/s and upload speeds of up to 5 Mbit/s. Cable internet is gaining more popularity with its higher download speeds ranging from 50 Mbit/s to 1 Gbit/s.

Two [computer security](/wiki/Computer_security "Computer security") companies, [Avast](/wiki/Avast "Avast") and [AVG](/wiki/AVG_Technologies "AVG Technologies"), were founded in the Czech Republic. In 2016, Avast led by [Pavel Baudiš](/wiki/Pavel_Baudi%C5%A1 "Pavel Baudiš") bought rival AVG for [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1.3 billion, together at the time, these companies had a user base of about 400 million people and 40% of the consumer market outside of China.[[161]](#cite_note-167)[[162]](#cite_note-168) Avast is the leading provider of antivirus software, with a 20.5% market share.[[163]](#cite_note-169)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Czech_Republic "Tourism in the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Burg_Karlstein_-_Karl%C5%A1tejn_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Burg_Karlstein_-_Karl%C5%A1tejn_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burg_Karlstein_-_Karl%C5%A1tejn_-_panoramio.jpg)

Medieval castle [Karlštejn](/wiki/Karl%C5%A1tejn "Karlštejn")

[Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") is the fifth most visited city in Europe after London, Paris, Istanbul and Rome.[[164]](#cite_note-Bremner_2015-170) In 2001, the total earnings from tourism reached 118 billion [CZK](/wiki/Czech_koruna "Czech koruna"), making up 5.5% of the country's [GNP](/wiki/Measures_of_national_income_and_output "Measures of national income and output") and 9% of its overall export earnings. The industry employs more than 110,000 people – over 1% of the population.[[165]](#cite_note-171)
Guidebooks and tourists reporting overcharging by taxi drivers and pickpocketing problems talk mainly about Prague, though the situation has improved recently.[[166]](#cite_note-172)[[167]](#cite_note-indy-173) Since 2005, Prague's mayor, [Pavel Bém](/wiki/Pavel_B%C3%A9m "Pavel Bém"), has worked to improve this reputation by cracking down on petty crime[[167]](#cite_note-indy-173) and, aside from these problems, Prague is a "safe" city.[[168]](#cite_note-174) The Czech Republic's crime rate is described by the United States State department as "low".[[169]](#cite_note-175)

The Czech Republic boasts [17 UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of World Heritage Sites in the Czech Republic"), 3 of them being transnational. As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit), further 13 sites are on the tentative list.[[170]](#cite_note-176)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Krumlov%2C_historick%C3%A9_j%C3%A1dro.jpg/250px-%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Krumlov%2C_historick%C3%A9_j%C3%A1dro.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Krumlov,_historick%C3%A9_j%C3%A1dro.jpg)

[Český Krumlov](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Krumlov "Český Krumlov")

Architectural heritage is an object of interest to visitors – it includes [castles and châteaux](/wiki/List_of_castles_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of castles in the Czech Republic") from different historical epochs, namely [Karlštejn](/wiki/Karl%C5%A1tejn "Karlštejn") Castle, [Český Krumlov](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%BD_Krumlov "Český Krumlov") and the [Lednice–Valtice Cultural Landscape](/wiki/Lednice%E2%80%93Valtice_Cultural_Landscape "Lednice–Valtice Cultural Landscape"). There are 12 [cathedrals](/wiki/List_of_cathedrals_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of cathedrals in the Czech Republic") and 15 churches elevated to the rank of [basilica](/wiki/Minor_basilica "Minor basilica") by the Pope, as well as many monasteries.

Away from the towns, areas such as [Bohemian Paradise](/wiki/Bohemian_Paradise "Bohemian Paradise"), [Bohemian Forest](/wiki/Bohemian_Forest "Bohemian Forest") and the [Giant Mountains](/wiki/Giant_Mountains "Giant Mountains") attract visitors seeking outdoor pursuits.

The country is also known for its various [museums](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of museums in the Czech Republic"), [puppetry](/wiki/Puppeteer "Puppeteer") and [marionette](/wiki/Marionette "Marionette") exhibitions that take part within larger puppet [festivals](/wiki/Theatre_festival "Theatre festival"), and [beer festivals](/wiki/Beer_festival "Beer festival").[[171]](#cite_note-177) [Aquapalace Prague](/wiki/Aquapalace_Prague "Aquapalace Prague") in [Čestlice](/wiki/%C4%8Cestlice "Čestlice") is the largest [water park](/wiki/Water_park "Water park") in the country.

### Science

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Heyrovsky_Jaroslav.jpg/250px-Heyrovsky_Jaroslav.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heyrovsky_Jaroslav.jpg)

Chemist [Jaroslav Heyrovský](/wiki/Jaroslav_Heyrovsk%C3%BD "Jaroslav Heyrovský"), Nobel Prize winner

The Czech lands have a long and well-documented history of scientific innovation.[[172]](#cite_note-178)[[173]](#cite_note-179) Today, the Czech Republic has a developed scientific community supported by the government,[[174]](#cite_note-180) industry,[[175]](#cite_note-181) and leading [universities](/wiki/Czech_Universities "Czech Universities").[[176]](#cite_note-182) Czech scientists are embedded members of the global scientific community.[[177]](#cite_note-183) They contribute annually to multiple international academic journals and collaborate with their colleagues across boundaries and fields.[[178]](#cite_note-184)[[179]](#cite_note-185)[[180]](#cite_note-186)[[181]](#cite_note-187) The Czech Republic was ranked 24th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2020 and 2021, up from 26th in 2019.[[182]](#cite_note-188)[[183]](#cite_note-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190)

Historically, the Czech lands, especially [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), have been the seat of scientific discovery going back to early modern times, including [Tycho Brahe](/wiki/Tycho_Brahe "Tycho Brahe"), [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus"), and [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler"). In 1784 the scientific community was first formally organized under the charter of the [Royal Czech Society of Sciences](/wiki/Royal_Czech_Society_of_Sciences "Royal Czech Society of Sciences"). Currently, this organization is known as the [Czech Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Czech_Academy_of_Sciences "Czech Academy of Sciences").[[185]](#cite_note-191) Similarly, the Czech lands have a well-established history of scientists,[[186]](#cite_note-192)[[187]](#cite_note-193) including Nobel laureates biochemists [Gerty](/wiki/Gerty_Cori "Gerty Cori") and [Carl Ferdinand Cori](/wiki/Carl_Ferdinand_Cori "Carl Ferdinand Cori"), chemists [Jaroslav Heyrovský](/wiki/Jaroslav_Heyrovsk%C3%BD "Jaroslav Heyrovský") (inventor of [polarography](/wiki/Polarography "Polarography"), [electroanalytical chemistry](/wiki/Analytical_chemistry "Analytical chemistry") and recipient of the Nobel Prize) and [Otto Wichterle](/wiki/Otto_Wichterle "Otto Wichterle") ([chemists](/wiki/Chemist "Chemist") responsible for the invention of the modern [contact lens](/wiki/Contact_lens "Contact lens") and silon-[synthetic fiber](/wiki/Synthetic_fiber "Synthetic fiber")), physicists [Ernst Mach](/wiki/Ernst_Mach "Ernst Mach") (physicist and critic of Newton's theories of space and time, foreshadowing Einstein's theory of relativity) and [Peter Grünberg](/wiki/Peter_Gr%C3%BCnberg "Peter Grünberg"), physiologist [Jan Evangelista Purkyně](/wiki/Jan_Evangelista_Purkyn%C4%9B "Jan Evangelista Purkyně") and chemist [Antonín Holý](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Hol%C3%BD "Antonín Holý") (scientist and chemist, in 2009 was involved in creation of the most effective drug in the treatment of AIDS),[[188]](#cite_note-194)[[189]](#cite_note-195) [Sigmund Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud"), the founder of [psychoanalysis](/wiki/Psychoanalysis "Psychoanalysis"), was born in [Příbor](/wiki/P%C5%99%C3%ADbor "Příbor"),[[190]](#cite_note-196) [Gregor Mendel](/wiki/Gregor_Mendel "Gregor Mendel"), the founder of [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), was born in [Hynčice](/wiki/Hyn%C4%8Dice_(Vra%C5%BEn%C3%A9) "Hynčice (Vražné)") and spent most of his life in [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"),[[191]](#cite_note-197) logician and mathematician [Kurt Gödel](/wiki/Kurt_G%C3%B6del "Kurt Gödel") was born in Brno.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/ELI_Beamlines_building_in_Doln%C3%AD_B%C5%99e%C5%BEany.jpg/250px-ELI_Beamlines_building_in_Doln%C3%AD_B%C5%99e%C5%BEany.jpg)](/wiki/File:ELI_Beamlines_building_in_Doln%C3%AD_B%C5%99e%C5%BEany.jpg)

Eli Beamlines Science Center with the most powerful laser in the world in [Dolní Břežany](/wiki/Doln%C3%AD_B%C5%99e%C5%BEany "Dolní Břežany")

Other famous Czech scientists include: [Stanislav Brebera](/wiki/Stanislav_Brebera "Stanislav Brebera") (inventor of the [plastic explosive](/wiki/Plastic_explosive "Plastic explosive") [Semtex](/wiki/Semtex "Semtex")),[[192]](#cite_note-198) [Václav Prokop Diviš](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Prokop_Divi%C5%A1 "Václav Prokop Diviš") (inventor of the first grounded [lightning rod](/wiki/Lightning_rod "Lightning rod")), [Jakub Husník](/wiki/Jakub_Husn%C3%ADk "Jakub Husník") (improved the process of [photolithography](/wiki/Photolithography "Photolithography")), [Jan Janský](/wiki/Jan_Jansk%C3%BD "Jan Janský") (serologist and neurologist, discovered the [ABO blood groups](/wiki/ABO_blood_groups "ABO blood groups")), [Karel Klíč](/wiki/Karel_Kl%C3%AD%C4%8D "Karel Klíč") (painter and photographer, inventor of the [photogravure](/wiki/Photogravure "Photogravure")), [František Křižík](/wiki/Franti%C5%A1ek_K%C5%99i%C5%BE%C3%ADk "František Křižík") (electrical engineer, inventor of the [arc lamp](/wiki/Arc_lamp "Arc lamp")), [Jan Marek Marci](/wiki/Jan_Marek_Marci "Jan Marek Marci") (mathematician, physicist and imperial physician, one of the founders of [spectroscopy](/wiki/Spectroscopy "Spectroscopy")),[[193]](#cite_note-199) [Vladimír Remek](/wiki/Vladim%C3%ADr_Remek "Vladimír Remek") (the first person outside of the Soviet Union and the United States to go into space), [Jakub Kryštof Rad](/wiki/Jakub_Kry%C5%A1tof_Rad "Jakub Kryštof Rad") (inventor of [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") cubes), [Josef Ressel](/wiki/Josef_Ressel "Josef Ressel") (inventor of the [screw propeller](/wiki/Propeller "Propeller") and [modern compass](/wiki/Protractor_compass "Protractor compass")).[[194]](#cite_note-science1-200)

Historically, most scientific research was recorded in Latin, but from the 18th century onwards increasingly in German and Czech, archived in libraries supported and managed by religious groups and other denominations, as evidenced by historical locations of international renown and heritage such as the [Strahov Monastery](/wiki/Strahov_Monastery "Strahov Monastery") and the [Clementinum](/wiki/Clementinum "Clementinum") in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). Increasingly, Czech scientists publish their work and that of their history in English.[[195]](#cite_note-201)[[196]](#cite_note-202)

Current important scientific institutions include the already mentioned [Academy of Sciences of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Academy_of_Sciences "Czech Academy of Sciences"), the [CEITEC](/wiki/Central_European_Institute_of_Technology "Central European Institute of Technology") Institute in [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno") and the [HiLASE](/w/index.php?title=HiLASE&action=edit&redlink=1 "HiLASE (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/HiLASE "cs:HiLASE")] and [Eli Beamlines](/w/index.php?title=Eli_Beamlines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eli Beamlines (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eli_Beamlines "cs:Eli Beamlines")] centers with the most powerful laser in the world in [Dolní Břežany](/wiki/Doln%C3%AD_B%C5%99e%C5%BEany "Dolní Břežany"). Prague is the seat of the administrative center of the GSA Agency operating the European navigation system [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") and the [European Union Agency for the Space Programme](/wiki/European_Union_Agency_for_the_Space_Programme "European Union Agency for the Space Programme").

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Czech_Republic "Demographics of the Czech Republic")

See also: [List of cities and towns in the Czech Republic](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of cities and towns in the Czech Republic")

The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) in 2020 was estimated at 1.71 children per woman, which is below the replacement rate of 2.1.[[197]](#cite_note-203) The Czech Republic's population has an average age of 43.3 years.[[198]](#cite_note-204) The life expectancy in 2021 was estimated to be 79.5 years (76.55 years male, 82.61 years female).[[199]](#cite_note-cia.gov-205) In 2024, there were 121,823 immigrants to and 84,978 emigrants from the Czech Republic, for a net migration of 36,845, of whom 17 thousand were Ukrainian and 4.7 thousand were Slovak.[[200]](#cite_note-206) [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people_in_the_Czech_Republic "Vietnamese people in the Czech Republic") immigrants began settling in the country during the Communist period, when they were invited as [guest workers](/wiki/Guest_workers "Guest workers") by the Czechoslovak government.[[201]](#cite_note-207) In 2009, there were about 70,000 Vietnamese in the Czech Republic.[[202]](#cite_note-208) Most have decided to stay in the country permanently.[[203]](#cite_note-209)

According to results of the 2021 census, the majority of the inhabitants of the Czech Republic are [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") (57.3%), followed by [Moravians](/wiki/Moravians_(ethnic_group) "Moravians (ethnic group)") (3.4%), [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") (0.9%), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") (0.7%), [Viets](/wiki/Vietnamese_people "Vietnamese people") (0.3%), [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") (0.3%), [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians") (0.2%), [Silesians](/wiki/Silesians "Silesians") (0.1%) and [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans") (0.1%). Another 4.0% declared a combination of two nationalities (3.6% combination of Czech and other nationality). As the 'nationality' was an optional item, a number of people left this field blank (31.6%).[[7]](#cite_note-census2021-9) According to some estimates, there are about 250,000 [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") in the Czech Republic.[[204]](#cite_note-210)[[205]](#cite_note-211) The [Polish minority](/wiki/Polish_minority_in_the_Czech_Republic "Polish minority in the Czech Republic") resides mainly in the [Trans-Olza](/wiki/Trans-Olza "Trans-Olza") region.[[206]](#cite_note-212)

There were 658,564 foreigners residing in the country in 2021,[[207]](#cite_note-213) according to the [Czech Statistical Office](/wiki/Czech_Statistical_Office "Czech Statistical Office"), with the largest groups being [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainians_in_the_Czech_Republic "Ukrainians in the Czech Republic") (22%), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovaks_in_the_Czech_Republic "Slovaks in the Czech Republic") (22%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people_in_the_Czech_Republic "Vietnamese people in the Czech Republic") (12%), [Russian](/wiki/Russians "Russians") (7%) and [German](/wiki/Germans_in_the_Czech_Republic "Germans in the Czech Republic") (4%). Most of the foreign population lives in Prague (37.3%) and Central Bohemia Region (13.2%).[[208]](#cite_note-214)

The [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_the_Czech_Republic "History of the Jews in the Czech Republic") population of Bohemia and Moravia, 118,000 according to the 1930 census, was nearly annihilated by the Nazi Germans during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[209]](#cite_note-215) There were approximately 3,900 Jews in the Czech Republic in 2021.[[210]](#cite_note-216) The former Czech prime minister, [Jan Fischer](/wiki/Jan_Fischer_(politician) "Jan Fischer (politician)"), is of Jewish faith.[[211]](#cite_note-217)

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Czech_Republic "Religion in the Czech Republic") and [Religion in the European Union](/wiki/Religion_in_the_European_Union "Religion in the European Union")

Religion in the Czech Republic (2011)[[215]](#cite_note-Czech_Statistical_Office-221)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | |  |  | |
| Undeclared | |  | 44.7% | |
| Irreligion | |  | 34.5% | |
| Catholicism | |  | 10.5% | |
| Believers, not members of other religions | |  | 6.8% | |
| Other Christian churches | |  | 1.1% | |
| Protestantism | |  | 1% | |
| Believers, members of other religions | |  | 0.7% | |
| Other religions / Unknown | |  | 0.7% | |

About 75%[[216]](#cite_note-222) to 79%[[217]](#cite_note-Czech_Statistical_Office_27_February_2014-223) of residents of the Czech Republic do not declare having any religion or faith in surveys, and the proportion of convinced [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") (30%) is the third highest in the world behind those of [China](/wiki/China "China") (47%) and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") (31%).[[218]](#cite_note-Global_Index_of_Religion_and_Atheism-224)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] The [Czech people](/wiki/Czech_people "Czech people") have been historically characterized as "tolerant and even indifferent towards religion".[[219]](#cite_note-225) The religious identity of the country has changed drastically since the first half of the 20th century, when more than 90% of [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") were Christians.[[220]](#cite_note-226)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Emma_Czeska_i_%C5%9Aw._Wac%C5%82aw.JPG/250px-Emma_Czeska_i_%C5%9Aw._Wac%C5%82aw.JPG)](/wiki/File:Emma_Czeska_i_%C5%9Aw._Wac%C5%82aw.JPG)

[Saint Wenceslaus](/wiki/Wenceslaus_I,_Duke_of_Bohemia "Wenceslaus I, Duke of Bohemia") (left), patron saint of the Czech lands

Christianization in the 9th and 10th centuries introduced [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). After the [Bohemian Reformation](/wiki/Bohemian_Reformation "Bohemian Reformation"), most Czechs became [followers](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") of [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus"), [Petr Chelčický](/wiki/Petr_Chel%C4%8Dick%C3%BD "Petr Chelčický") and other regional [Protestant Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers"). [Taborites](/wiki/Taborites "Taborites") and [Utraquists](/wiki/Utraquists "Utraquists") were [Hussite](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite") groups. Towards the end of the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"), the Utraquists changed sides and allied with the Roman Catholic Church. Following the joint Utraquist—Roman Catholic victory, Utraquism was accepted as a distinct form of Christianity to be practiced in [Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia") by the Roman Catholic Church while all remaining Hussite groups were prohibited. After the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), some Bohemians went with the [teachings of Martin Luther](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), especially [Sudeten Germans](/wiki/Sudeten_Germans "Sudeten Germans"). In the wake of the Reformation, Utraquist Hussites took a renewed increasingly [anti-Catholic](/wiki/Anti-Catholic "Anti-Catholic") stance, while some of the defeated Hussite factions were revived. After the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs") regained control of Bohemia, the whole population was forcibly converted to Roman Catholicism—even the Utraquist Hussites. Going forward, Czechs have become more wary and pessimistic of religion as such. A history of resistance to the Roman Catholic Church followed. It suffered a schism with the neo-Hussite [Czechoslovak Hussite Church](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Hussite_Church "Czechoslovak Hussite Church") in 1920, lost the bulk of its adherents during the Communist era and continues to lose in the modern, ongoing [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization"). Protestantism never recovered after the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") was introduced by the [Austrian Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") in 1620. Prior to the Holocaust, the Czech Republic had a sizable Jewish community of around 100,000. There are many historically important and culturally relevant Synagogues in the Czech Republic such as Europe's oldest active Synagogue, [The Old New Synagogue](/wiki/Old_New_Synagogue "Old New Synagogue") and the second largest Synagogue in Europe, the [Great Synagogue (Plzeň)](/wiki/Great_Synagogue_(Plze%C5%88) "Great Synagogue (Plzeň)"). The Holocaust decimated Czech Jewry and the Jewish population as of 2021 is 3,900.[[221]](#cite_note-227)

According to the 2011 census, 34% of the population stated they had no religion, 10.3% were Roman Catholic, 0.8% were [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") (0.5% [Czech Brethren](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_of_Czech_Brethren "Evangelical Church of Czech Brethren") and 0.4% [Hussite](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Hussite_Church "Czechoslovak Hussite Church")),[[222]](#cite_note-228) and 9% followed other forms of religion both denominational or not (of which 863 people answered they are [Pagan](/wiki/Neopaganism "Neopaganism")). 45% of the population did not answer the question about religion.[[215]](#cite_note-Czech_Statistical_Office-221) From 1991 to 2001 and further to 2011 the adherence to Roman Catholicism decreased from 39% to 27% and then to 10%; Protestantism similarly declined from 3.7% to 2% and then to 0.8%.[[223]](#cite_note-229) The Muslim population is estimated to be 20,000 representing 0.2% of the population.[[224]](#cite_note-230)

The proportion of religious believers varies significantly across the country, from 55% in [Zlín Region](/wiki/Zl%C3%ADn_Region "Zlín Region") to 16% in [Ústí nad Labem Region](/wiki/%C3%9Ast%C3%AD_nad_Labem_Region "Ústí nad Labem Region").[[225]](#cite_note-231)

### Education and health care

Main articles: [Education in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Education_in_the_Czech_Republic "Education in the Czech Republic") and [Healthcare in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Healthcare_in_the_Czech_Republic "Healthcare in the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Praha_Karolinum_v%C3%BDklenek_1.jpg/250px-Praha_Karolinum_v%C3%BDklenek_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Praha_Karolinum_v%C3%BDklenek_1.jpg)

The oldest part of [Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University"), founded in 1348

Education in the Czech Republic is compulsory for nine years and citizens have access to a [free-tuition](/wiki/Free_education "Free education") [university education](/wiki/Tertiary_education_in_the_Czech_Republic "Tertiary education in the Czech Republic"), while the average number of years of education is 13.1.[[226]](#cite_note-Sign_In-232) Additionally, the Czech Republic has a "relatively equal" educational system in comparison with other countries in Europe.[[226]](#cite_note-Sign_In-232) Founded in 1348, [Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University") was the first university in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"). Other major universities in the country are [Masaryk University](/wiki/Masaryk_University "Masaryk University"), [Czech Technical University](/wiki/Czech_Technical_University_in_Prague "Czech Technical University in Prague"), [Palacký University](/wiki/Palack%C3%BD_University,_Olomouc "Palacký University, Olomouc"), [Academy of Performing Arts](/wiki/Academy_of_Performing_Arts_in_Prague "Academy of Performing Arts in Prague") and [University of Economics](/wiki/University_of_Economics,_Prague "University of Economics, Prague").

The [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment"), coordinated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), currently ranks the Czech education system as the 15th most successful in the world, higher than the OECD average.[[227]](#cite_note-233) The UN [Education Index](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index") ranks the Czech Republic 10th as of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit) (positioned behind [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and ahead of [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea")).[[228]](#cite_note-234)

Health care in the Czech Republic is similar in quality to that of other developed nations. The Czech [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") system is based on a [compulsory insurance model](/wiki/Health_insurance_mandate "Health insurance mandate"), with [fee-for-service](/wiki/Fee-for-service "Fee-for-service") care funded by mandatory employment-related insurance plans.[[229]](#cite_note-primary-235) According to the 2016 [Euro health consumer index](/wiki/Euro_health_consumer_index "Euro health consumer index"), a comparison of [healthcare in Europe](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Europe "Healthcare in Europe"), the Czech healthcare is 13th, ranked behind [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and two positions ahead of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[230]](#cite_note-EHCI_2016-236)

### Art

Main article: [Czech art](/wiki/Czech_art "Czech art")

[![Painting of a woman](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Spring.jpg/120px-Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Spring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Spring.jpg)

[![Painting of a woman](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Summer.jpg/120px-Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Summer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Summer.jpg)

[![Painting of a woman](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Autumn.jpg/120px-Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Autumn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Autumn.jpg)

[![Painting of a woman](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Winter.jpg/120px-Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Winter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfons_Mucha_-_1896_-_Winter.jpg)

*Spring*, *Summer*, *Autumn* and *Winter* (1896) by [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") artist [Alphonse Mucha](/wiki/Alphonse_Mucha "Alphonse Mucha")

[Venus of Dolní Věstonice](/wiki/Venus_of_Doln%C3%AD_V%C4%9Bstonice "Venus of Dolní Věstonice") is an important example of prehistoric art unearthed in the Czech Republic. [Theodoric of Prague](/wiki/Theodoric_of_Prague "Theodoric of Prague") was a painter in the Gothic era who decorated the castle [Karlštejn](/wiki/Karl%C5%A1tejn "Karlštejn"). In the Baroque era, there were painters [Wenceslaus Hollar](/wiki/Wenceslaus_Hollar "Wenceslaus Hollar"), [Jan Kupecký](/wiki/Jan_Kupeck%C3%BD "Jan Kupecký"), [Karel Škréta](/wiki/Karel_%C5%A0kr%C3%A9ta "Karel Škréta"), [Anton Raphael Mengs](/wiki/Anton_Raphael_Mengs "Anton Raphael Mengs") and [Petr Brandl](/wiki/Petr_Brandl "Petr Brandl") and sculptors [Matthias Braun](/wiki/Matthias_Braun "Matthias Braun") and [Ferdinand Brokoff](/wiki/Ferdinand_Brokoff "Ferdinand Brokoff").

In the first half of the 19th century, [Josef Mánes](/wiki/Josef_M%C3%A1nes "Josef Mánes") joined the romantic movement. In the second half the so-called "National Theatre generation" rose to prominence: sculptor [Josef Václav Myslbek](/wiki/Josef_V%C3%A1clav_Myslbek "Josef Václav Myslbek") and painters [Mikoláš Aleš](/wiki/Mikol%C3%A1%C5%A1_Ale%C5%A1 "Mikoláš Aleš"), [Václav Brožík](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Bro%C5%BE%C3%ADk "Václav Brožík"), [Vojtěch Hynais](/wiki/Vojt%C4%9Bch_Hynais "Vojtěch Hynais") and [Julius Mařák](/wiki/Julius_Ma%C5%99%C3%A1k "Julius Mařák"). At the end of the century came [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau"), with [Alfons Mucha](/wiki/Alphonse_Mucha "Alphonse Mucha") becoming its main representative. He is known for his Art Nouveau posters and a cycle of 20 large canvases named [the Slav Epic](/wiki/The_Slav_Epic "The Slav Epic"), which depicts the history of Czechs and other [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"). As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Czech_Republic&action=edit), it can be seen in the Veletržní Palace of the [National Gallery in Prague](/wiki/National_Gallery_in_Prague "National Gallery in Prague"), which manages the largest collection of art in the Czech Republic. [Max Švabinský](/wiki/Max_%C5%A0vabinsk%C3%BD "Max Švabinský") was another Art Nouveau painter.

The 20th century brought an [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") revolution, represented in the Czech lands mainly by expressionists and cubists: [Josef Čapek](/wiki/Josef_%C4%8Capek "Josef Čapek"), [Emil Filla](/wiki/Emil_Filla "Emil Filla"), [Bohumil Kubišta](/wiki/Bohumil_Kubi%C5%A1ta "Bohumil Kubišta") or [Jan Zrzavý](/wiki/Jan_Zrzav%C3%BD "Jan Zrzavý"). Surrealism emerged particularly through the work of [Toyen](/wiki/Toyen "Toyen"), [Josef Šíma](/wiki/Josef_%C5%A0%C3%ADma "Josef Šíma") and [Karel Teige](/wiki/Karel_Teige "Karel Teige"). In the world, however, the most well-known Czech avant-garde artist might be [František Kupka](/wiki/Franti%C5%A1ek_Kupka "František Kupka"), a pioneer of abstract painting. Illustrators and cartoonists to gain fame in the first half of the 20th century include [Josef Lada](/wiki/Josef_Lada "Josef Lada"), [Zdeněk Burian](/wiki/Zden%C4%9Bk_Burian "Zdeněk Burian") or [Emil Orlík](/wiki/Emil_Orl%C3%ADk "Emil Orlík"). Art photography became a new field represented by [František Drtikol](/wiki/Franti%C5%A1ek_Drtikol "František Drtikol"), [Josef Sudek](/wiki/Josef_Sudek "Josef Sudek"), later [Jan Saudek](/wiki/Jan_Saudek "Jan Saudek") and [Josef Koudelka](/wiki/Josef_Koudelka "Josef Koudelka").

The Czech Republic is also known for its individually made, mouth-blown, and decorated [Bohemian glass](/wiki/Bohemian_glass "Bohemian glass").

### Architecture

Main article: [Czech architecture](/wiki/Czech_architecture "Czech architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Prague_07-2016_View_from_Old_Town_Hall_Tower_img3.jpg/250px-Prague_07-2016_View_from_Old_Town_Hall_Tower_img3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prague_07-2016_View_from_Old_Town_Hall_Tower_img3.jpg)

[Historic center of Prague](/wiki/Old_Town_(Prague) "Old Town (Prague)")

The earliest preserved stone buildings in Bohemia and Moravia date back to the time of the [Christianization](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization") in the 9th and 10th centuries. Since the Middle Ages, the Czech lands have been using the same architectural styles as most of [Western](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and Central Europe. The oldest still standing churches were built in the [Romanesque style](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture"). During the 13th century, it was replaced by the [Gothic style](/wiki/Czech_Gothic_architecture "Czech Gothic architecture"). In the 14th century, Emperor Charles IV invited architects from France and Germany, [Matthias of Arras](/wiki/Matthias_of_Arras "Matthias of Arras") and [Peter Parler](/wiki/Peter_Parler "Peter Parler"), to his court in Prague. During the Middle Ages, some fortified castles were built by the king and aristocracy, as well as some monasteries.

The [Renaissance style](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") penetrated the Bohemian Crown in the late 15th century when the older Gothic style started to be mixed with Renaissance elements. An example of pure Renaissance architecture in Bohemia is the [Queen Anne's Summer Palace](/wiki/Queen_Anne%27s_Summer_Palace "Queen Anne's Summer Palace"), which was situated in the garden of [Prague Castle](/wiki/Prague_Castle "Prague Castle"). Evidence of the general reception of the Renaissance in Bohemia, involving an influx of Italian architects, can be found in spacious chateaus with arcade courtyards and geometrically arranged gardens.[[231]](#cite_note-architecture-237) Emphasis was placed on comfort, and buildings that were built for entertainment purposes also appeared.[[232]](#cite_note-czecharchitecture-238)

In the 17th century, the Baroque style spread throughout the Crown of Bohemia.[[233]](#cite_note-kotalik-239)

In the 18th century, Bohemia produced an architectural peculiarity – the *Baroque Gothic style*, a synthesis of the Gothic and Baroque styles.[[231]](#cite_note-architecture-237)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Prag_Kubismus_Schwarze_Madonna_1.jpg/250px-Prag_Kubismus_Schwarze_Madonna_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prag_Kubismus_Schwarze_Madonna_1.jpg)

Czech artists developed a [distinct cubist style](/wiki/Czech_Cubism "Czech Cubism") in architecture and applied arts. It later evolved into national Czechoslovak style, *[rondocubism](/wiki/Rondocubism "Rondocubism")*.

During the 19th century stands the [revival architectural styles](/wiki/Revival_architecture "Revival architecture"). Some churches were restored to their presumed medieval appearance and there were constructed buildings in the [Neo-Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture"), [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") and [Neo-Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_Revival_architecture "Renaissance Revival architecture") styles. At the turn of the 19th and 20th centuries, the new art style appeared in the Czech lands – [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau").

Bohemia contributed an unusual style to the world's architectural heritage when Czech architects attempted to transpose the [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism") of painting and sculpture into architecture.

Between World Wars I and II, [Functionalism](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)"), with its sober, progressive forms, took over as the main architectural style.[[231]](#cite_note-architecture-237)

After World War II and the Communist coup in 1948, art in Czechoslovakia became Soviet-influenced. The Czechoslovak avant-garde artistic movement is known as the *[Brussels style](/w/index.php?title=Brussels_style&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brussels style (page does not exist)") came up* in the time of political liberalization of Czechoslovakia in the 1960s. [Brutalism](/wiki/Brutalism "Brutalism") dominated in the 1970s and 1980s.

The Czech Republic is not shying away from the more modern trends of international architecture, an example is the [Dancing House (Tančící dům)](/wiki/Tan%C4%8D%C3%ADc%C3%AD_d%C5%AFm "Tančící dům") in Prague, [Golden Angel](/wiki/Golden_Angel "Golden Angel") in Prague or Congress Centre in Zlín.[[231]](#cite_note-architecture-237)

### Literature

Main article: [Czech literature](/wiki/Czech_literature "Czech literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Kafka.jpg/250px-Kafka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kafka.jpg)

[Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka")

The literature from the area of today's Czech Republic was mostly written in Czech, but also in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and German or even [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic"). [Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka"), although a competent user of Czech, wrote in his mother tongue, German.[[234]](#cite_note-240)[[235]](#cite_note-241) His works include *[The Trial](/wiki/The_Trial "The Trial")* and *[The Castle](/wiki/The_Castle_(novel) "The Castle (novel)")*.

In the second half of the 13th century, the royal court in Prague became one of the centers of German [Minnesang](/wiki/Minnesang "Minnesang") and courtly literature. The Czech German-language literature can be seen in the first half of the 20th century.

[Bible translations](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_Czech "Bible translations into Czech") played a role in the development of Czech literature. The oldest Czech translation of the [Psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms") originated in the late 13th century and the first complete Czech translation of the Bible was finished around 1360. The first complete printed Czech Bible was published in 1488. The first complete Czech Bible translation from the original languages was published between 1579 and 1593. The [Codex Gigas](/wiki/Codex_Gigas "Codex Gigas") from the 12th century is the largest extant medieval manuscript in the world.[[236]](#cite_note-242)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Jaroslav_Seifert_1981_foto_Hana_Hamplov%C3%A1_crop.jpg/250px-Jaroslav_Seifert_1981_foto_Hana_Hamplov%C3%A1_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jaroslav_Seifert_1981_foto_Hana_Hamplov%C3%A1_crop.jpg)

[Jaroslav Seifert](/wiki/Jaroslav_Seifert "Jaroslav Seifert"), winner of the Nobel Prize for Literature

Czech-language literature can be divided into several periods: the Middle Ages; the Hussite period; the [Renaissance humanism](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism"); the Baroque period; the Enlightenment and Czech reawakening in the first half of the 19th century, modern literature in the second half of the 19th century; the avant-garde of the interwar period; the years under Communism; and the Czech Republic.

The antiwar comedy novel *[The Good Soldier Švejk](/wiki/The_Good_Soldier_%C5%A0vejk "The Good Soldier Švejk")* is the most translated Czech book in history.

The international literary award the [Franz Kafka Prize](/wiki/Franz_Kafka_Prize "Franz Kafka Prize") is awarded in the Czech Republic.[[237]](#cite_note-243)

The Czech Republic has the densest network of [libraries](/wiki/List_of_libraries_in_the_Czech_Republic "List of libraries in the Czech Republic") in Europe.[[238]](#cite_note-244)

Czech literature and culture played a role on at least two occasions when Czechs lived under oppression and political activity was suppressed. On both of these occasions, in the early 19th century and then again in the 1960s, the Czechs used their cultural and literary effort to strive for political freedom, establishing a confident, politically aware nation.[[239]](#cite_note-245)

### Music

Main articles: [Music of the Czech Lands](/wiki/Music_of_the_Czech_Lands "Music of the Czech Lands") and [Moravian traditional music](/wiki/Moravian_traditional_music "Moravian traditional music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Dvorak.jpg/250px-Dvorak.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dvorak.jpg)

[Antonín Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák")

The musical tradition of the Czech lands arose from the first church hymns, whose first evidence is suggested at the break of the 10th and 11th centuries. Some pieces of Czech music include two chorales, which in their time performed the function of anthems: "[Lord, Have Mercy on Us](/wiki/Hospodine_pomiluj_ny "Hospodine pomiluj ny")" and the [hymn](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") "Saint Wenceslaus" or "[Saint Wenceslaus Chorale](/wiki/Saint_Wenceslas_Chorale "Saint Wenceslas Chorale")".[[240]](#cite_note-246) The authorship of the anthem "Lord, Have Mercy on Us" is ascribed by some historians to [Saint Adalbert of Prague](/wiki/Saint_Adalbert_of_Prague "Saint Adalbert of Prague") (sv.Vojtěch), bishop of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), living between 956 and 997.[[241]](#cite_note-247)

The wealth of musical culture lies in the classical music tradition during all historical periods, especially in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music"), [Classicism](/wiki/Classical_period_(music) "Classical period (music)"), Romantic, [modern classical](/wiki/Modernism_(music) "Modernism (music)") music and in the [traditional](/wiki/Traditional_music "Traditional music") folk music of Bohemia, Moravia and [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"). Since the early era of artificial music, Czech musicians and composers have been influenced the folk music of the region and dance.

Czech music can be considered to have been contributed strongly to the development of music in both the European and worldwide contexts, having several times co-determined or even determined a newly arriving era in musical art,[[242]](#cite_note-248) above all of the [Classical](/wiki/Classical_period_(music) "Classical period (music)") era, as well as by original attitudes in [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_(music) "Baroque (music)"), [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_(music) "Romantic (music)") and [modern classical](/wiki/Modern_classical "Modern classical") music. Czech music burst onto the world stage in the romantic era beginning with the works of [Bedřich Smetana](/wiki/Bed%C5%99ich_Smetana "Bedřich Smetana") who is often considered the 'father of Czech music'. Some Czech musical works are *[The Bartered Bride](/wiki/The_Bartered_Bride "The Bartered Bride")* (Smetana), *[New World Symphony](/wiki/New_World_Symphony "New World Symphony")* (Dvořák), and *[Sinfonietta](/wiki/Sinfonietta_(Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek) "Sinfonietta (Janáček)")* and *[Jenůfa](/wiki/Jen%C5%AFfa "Jenůfa")* by [Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček").

A music festival in the country is [Prague Spring International Music Festival](/wiki/Prague_Spring_International_Music_Festival "Prague Spring International Music Festival") of classical music, a permanent showcase for performing artists, symphony orchestras and chamber music ensembles of the world.

### Theatre

Main article: [Theatre of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Theatre_of_the_Czech_Republic "Theatre of the Czech Republic")

[![Portrait](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Narodni_Divadlo%2C_National_Theater%2C_Prague_-_8798.jpg/250px-Narodni_Divadlo%2C_National_Theater%2C_Prague_-_8798.jpg)](/wiki/File:Narodni_Divadlo,_National_Theater,_Prague_-_8798.jpg)

[![Portrait](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Narodni_Divadlo%2C_Estates_Theater%2C_Prague_-_8604.jpg/120px-Narodni_Divadlo%2C_Estates_Theater%2C_Prague_-_8604.jpg)](/wiki/File:Narodni_Divadlo,_Estates_Theater,_Prague_-_8604.jpg)

The [National Theatre](/wiki/National_Theatre_(Prague) "National Theatre (Prague)") (left) and the [Estates Theatre](/wiki/Estates_Theatre "Estates Theatre") (right)

The roots of Czech theatre can be found in the Middle Ages, especially in the cultural life of the [Gothic period](/wiki/Gothic_period "Gothic period"). In the 19th century, the theatre played a role in the national awakening movement and later, in the 20th century, it became a part of modern European theatre art. The original Czech cultural phenomenon came into being at the end of the 1950s. This project called [Laterna magika](/wiki/Laterna_magika "Laterna magika"), resulting in productions that combined theater, dance, and film in a poetic manner, considered the first [multimedia art](/wiki/Multimedia_art "Multimedia art") project in an international context.

One drama is [Karel Čapek](/wiki/Karel_%C4%8Capek "Karel Čapek")'s play *[R.U.R.](/wiki/R.U.R. "R.U.R.")*, which introduced the word "[robot](/wiki/Robot "Robot")".[[243]](#cite_note-249)

The country has a tradition of [puppet theater](/wiki/Puppetry "Puppetry"). In 2016, [Czech and Slovak Puppetry](/wiki/Puppetry#Czech_Republic_and_Slovakia "Puppetry") was included on the [UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists").[[244]](#cite_note-250)

### Film

Main article: [Cinema of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_Czech_Republic "Cinema of the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Filmov%C3%BD_uzel_Zl%C3%ADn%2C_Herm%C3%ADna_T%C3%BDrlov%C3%A1_%281%29.jpg/250px-Filmov%C3%BD_uzel_Zl%C3%ADn%2C_Herm%C3%ADna_T%C3%BDrlov%C3%A1_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Filmov%C3%BD_uzel_Zl%C3%ADn,_Herm%C3%ADna_T%C3%BDrlov%C3%A1_(1).jpg)

[Hermína Týrlová](/wiki/Herm%C3%ADna_T%C3%BDrlov%C3%A1 "Hermína Týrlová"), animator and film director

The tradition of Czech cinematography started in the second half of the 1890s. Peaks of the production in the era of silent movies include the historical drama *The Builder of the Temple* and the social and erotic drama *Erotikon* directed by [Gustav Machatý](/wiki/Gustav_Machat%C3%BD "Gustav Machatý").[[245]](#cite_note-251) The early Czech [sound film](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") era was productive, above all in mainstream genres, with the comedies of [Martin Frič](/wiki/Martin_Fri%C4%8D "Martin Frič") or [Karel Lamač](/wiki/Karel_Lama%C4%8D "Karel Lamač"). There were dramatic movies sought internationally.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Hermína Týrlová](/wiki/Herm%C3%ADna_T%C3%BDrlov%C3%A1 "Hermína Týrlová") was a prominent Czech animator, screenwriter, and film director. She was often called the mother of Czech animation. Over the course of her career, she produced over 60 animated children's short films using puppets and the technique of stop motion animation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Before the German occupation, in 1933, filmmaker and animator [Irena Dodalová](/w/index.php?title=Irena_Dodalov%C3%A1&action=edit&redlink=1 "Irena Dodalová (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Irena_Dodalov%C3%A1 "cs:Irena Dodalová")] established the first Czech animation studio "IRE Film" with her husband Karel Dodal.[[246]](#cite_note-252)

After the period of Nazi occupation and early communist official dramaturgy of socialist realism in movies at the turn of the 1940s and 1950s with fewer exceptions such as *[Krakatit](/wiki/Krakatit "Krakatit")* or *Men without wings* (awarded by [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") in 1946), an era of the Czech film began with animated films, performed in anglophone countries under the name "The Fabulous World of Jules Verne" from 1958, which combined acted drama with animation, and [Jiří Trnka](/wiki/Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Trnka "Jiří Trnka"), the founder of the modern puppet film.[[247]](#cite_note-253) This began a tradition of animated films (*[Mole](/wiki/Mole_(Zden%C4%9Bk_Miler_character) "Mole (Zdeněk Miler character)")* etc.).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Milos_Forman.jpg/250px-Milos_Forman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milos_Forman.jpg)

Oscar-winning director [Miloš Forman](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1_Forman "Miloš Forman")

In the 1960s, the hallmark of [Czechoslovak New Wave](/wiki/Czechoslovak_New_Wave "Czechoslovak New Wave")'s films were improvised [dialogues](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue"), [black](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") and [absurd](/wiki/Absurdity "Absurdity") humor and the occupation of non-actors. Directors are trying to preserve natural atmosphere without refinement and artificial arrangement of scenes. A personality of the 1960s and the beginning of the 1970s with original manuscript and psychological impact is [František Vláčil](/wiki/Franti%C5%A1ek_Vl%C3%A1%C4%8Dil "František Vláčil"). Another international author is [Jan Švankmajer](/wiki/Jan_%C5%A0vankmajer "Jan Švankmajer"), a filmmaker and artist whose work spans several media. He is a self-labeled [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") known for animations and features.[[248]](#cite_note-254)

The [Barrandov Studios](/wiki/Barrandov_Studios "Barrandov Studios") in Prague are the largest film studios with film locations in the country.[[249]](#cite_note-255) Filmmakers have come to [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") to shoot scenery no longer found in Berlin, Paris and Vienna. The city of [Karlovy Vary](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary "Karlovy Vary") was used as a location for the 2006 James Bond film Casino Royale.[[250]](#cite_note-256)

The [Czech Lion](/wiki/Czech_Lion "Czech Lion") is the highest Czech award for film achievement. [Karlovy Vary International Film Festival](/wiki/Karlovy_Vary_International_Film_Festival "Karlovy Vary International Film Festival") is one of the film festivals that have been given competitive status by the [FIAPF](/wiki/FIAPF "FIAPF"). Other film festivals held in the country include [Febiofest](/wiki/Febiofest "Febiofest"), [Jihlava International Documentary Film Festival](/wiki/Jihlava_International_Documentary_Film_Festival "Jihlava International Documentary Film Festival"), [One World Film Festival](/wiki/One_World_Film_Festival "One World Film Festival"), [Zlín Film Festival](/wiki/Zl%C3%ADn_Film_Festival "Zlín Film Festival") and [Fresh Film Festival](/wiki/Fresh_Film_Festival "Fresh Film Festival").

### Media

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Kav%C4%8D%C3%AD_hory_are%C3%A1l_%C4%8CT_4.jpg/250px-Kav%C4%8D%C3%AD_hory_are%C3%A1l_%C4%8CT_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kav%C4%8D%C3%AD_hory_are%C3%A1l_%C4%8CT_4.jpg)

Headquarters of [Czech Television](/wiki/Czech_Television "Czech Television")

Czech journalists and media enjoy a degree of [freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press"). There are restrictions against writing in support of [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"), racism or violating [Czech law](/wiki/Czech_law "Czech law"). The Czech press was ranked as the 40th most free press in the World Press Freedom Index by [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders") in 2021.[[251]](#cite_note-257) [Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty](/wiki/Radio_Free_Europe/Radio_Liberty "Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty") has its headquarters in Prague.

[Czech Television](/wiki/Czech_Television "Czech Television") is the country's national public television broadcaster. It operates a number of channels, including [ČT1](/wiki/%C4%8CT1 "ČT1"), [ČT2](/wiki/%C4%8CT2 "ČT2"), and the 24-hour news channel [ČT24](/wiki/%C4%8CT24 "ČT24"), as well as the news website ct24.cz.[[252]](#cite_note-258) As of 2020, it is the most watched broadcaster, followed by the private [TV Nova](/wiki/TV_Nova_(Czech_Republic) "TV Nova (Czech Republic)") and [Prima TV](/wiki/Prima_televize "Prima televize"). However, TV Nova features the most watched main [news program](/wiki/News_program "News program") and [prime time](/wiki/Prime_time "Prime time") program.[[253]](#cite_note-259) Other public media services include the [Czech Radio](/wiki/Czech_Radio "Czech Radio") and the [Czech News Agency](/wiki/Czech_News_Agency "Czech News Agency").

The best-selling daily national newspapers in 2020/21 are [Blesk](/wiki/Blesk "Blesk") (average 703,000 daily readers), [Mladá fronta DNES](/wiki/Mlad%C3%A1_fronta_DNES "Mladá fronta DNES") (average 461,000 daily readers), [Právo](/wiki/Pr%C3%A1vo "Právo") (average 182,000 daily readers), [Lidové noviny](/wiki/Lidov%C3%A9_noviny "Lidové noviny") (average 163,000 daily readers) and [Hospodářské noviny](/wiki/Hospod%C3%A1%C5%99sk%C3%A9_noviny "Hospodářské noviny") (average 162,000 daily readers).[[254]](#cite_note-260)

Most Czechs (87%[[255]](#cite_note-261)) read their news online,[[256]](#cite_note-262) with [Seznam.cz](/wiki/Seznam.cz "Seznam.cz"), [iDNES.cz](/wiki/IDNES.cz "IDNES.cz"), [Novinky.cz](/wiki/Novinky.cz "Novinky.cz"), [iPrima.cz](/wiki/Prima_televize "Prima televize") and [Seznam Zprávy.cz](/wiki/Seznam_Zpr%C3%A1vy "Seznam Zprávy") being the most visited as of 2021.[[257]](#cite_note-263)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Czech cuisine](/wiki/Czech_cuisine "Czech cuisine")

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(July 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Pilsner_urquell_mug.jpg/250px-Pilsner_urquell_mug.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pilsner_urquell_mug.jpg)

A mug of [Pilsner Urquell](/wiki/Pilsner_Urquell "Pilsner Urquell"), the first [pilsner](/wiki/Pilsner "Pilsner") type of [pale lager](/wiki/Pale_lager "Pale lager") beer, brewed since 1842

Czech cuisine is marked by an emphasis on meat dishes with pork, beef, and chicken. Goose, duck, rabbit, and venison are served. Fish is less common, with the occasional exception of fresh [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout") and [carp](/wiki/Carp "Carp"), which is served at Christmas.[[258]](#cite_note-264)[[259]](#cite_note-265) One popular Czech menu item is *smažený vepřový řízek* (fried breaded pork filet), served with boiled potatoes.[[260]](#cite_note-266)

There is a variety of local sausages, wurst, pâtés, and smoked and cured meats. Czech desserts include a variety of whipped cream, chocolate, and fruit pastries and tarts, crêpes, creme desserts and cheese, poppy-seed-filled and other types of traditional cakes such as *[buchty](/wiki/Buchteln "Buchteln")*, *[koláče](/wiki/Kolach_(cake) "Kolach (cake)")* and [*štrúdl*](/wiki/Apple_strudel "Apple strudel").[[261]](#cite_note-267)

[Czech beer](/wiki/Beer_in_the_Czech_Republic "Beer in the Czech Republic") has a history extending more than a millennium; the earliest known brewery existed in 993. Today, the Czech Republic has the highest [beer consumption per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_beer_consumption_per_capita "List of countries by beer consumption per capita") in the world. The [pilsner](/wiki/Pilsner "Pilsner") style beer (pils) originated in [Plzeň](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň"), where the world's first blond lager [Pilsner Urquell](/wiki/Pilsner_Urquell "Pilsner Urquell") is still produced. It has served as the inspiration for more than two-thirds of the beer produced in the world today. The city of [České Budějovice](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_Bud%C4%9Bjovice "České Budějovice") has similarly lent its name to its beer, known as [Budweiser Budvar](/wiki/Budweiser_Budvar "Budweiser Budvar").

The South Moravian region has been producing [wine](/wiki/Czech_wine "Czech wine") since the Middle Ages; about 94% of [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard") in the Czech Republic are Moravian. Aside from beer, [slivovitz](/wiki/Slivovitz "Slivovitz") and wine, the Czech Republic also produces two [liquors](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor"), [Fernet Stock](/wiki/Fernet_Stock "Fernet Stock") and [Becherovka](/wiki/Becherovka "Becherovka"). [Kofola](/wiki/Kofola "Kofola") is a non-alcoholic domestic [cola](/wiki/Cola "Cola") soft drink which competes with [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") and [Pepsi](/wiki/Pepsi "Pepsi").

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Sport_in_the_Czech_Republic "Sport in the Czech Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/JaromirJagr2010WinterOlympics.jpg/250px-JaromirJagr2010WinterOlympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:JaromirJagr2010WinterOlympics.jpg)

Hockey player [Jaromír Jágr](/wiki/Jarom%C3%ADr_J%C3%A1gr "Jaromír Jágr") has the second-most [points](/wiki/Point_(ice_hockey) "Point (ice hockey)") in NHL history, after [Wayne Gretzky](/wiki/Wayne_Gretzky "Wayne Gretzky").[[262]](#cite_note-268)

The most watched and most attended sport in the Czech Republic are [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey").[[263]](#cite_note-269) The most watched sporting events are the [Ice hockey at the Olympic Games](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games") and the [Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships").[[264]](#cite_note-270)[[265]](#cite_note-271) The most popular sports in the Czech Republic according to the size of the membership base of sports clubs are: football, [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), ice hockey, [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), [floorball](/wiki/Floorball "Floorball"), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [ball hockey](/wiki/Ball_hockey "Ball hockey"), [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") and [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing").[[266]](#cite_note-272)

The country has won 15 gold medals in the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") and nine in the [Winter Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"). (See [Olympic history](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Olympic Games medal table").) The [Czech ice hockey team](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") won the gold medal at the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") and has won (along with the [Czechoslovak](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czechoslovakia men's national ice hockey team") team) thirteen gold medals at the [World Championships](/wiki/IHWC "IHWC"), including three straight from [1999](/wiki/1999_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1999 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships") to [2001](/wiki/2001_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2001 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships").[[267]](#cite_note-273)

The [Škoda Motorsport](/wiki/%C5%A0koda_Motorsport "Škoda Motorsport") is engaged in [competition racing](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport") since 1901 and has gained a number of titles with various vehicles around the world. [MTX](/wiki/MTX_(automobile) "MTX (automobile)") automobile company was formerly engaged in the manufacture of [racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing") and [formula](/wiki/Formula_racing "Formula racing") cars since 1969.[[268]](#cite_note-274)

[Hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") is a popular sport. The word for 'tourist' in Czech, *turista*, also means 'trekker' or 'hiker'. For hikers, thanks to the more than 120-year-old tradition, there is the [Czech Hiking Markers System](/wiki/Czech_Hiking_Markers_System "Czech Hiking Markers System") of [trail blazing](/wiki/Trail_blazing "Trail blazing"), that has been adopted by countries worldwide. There is a network of around 40,000 km of marked short- and long-distance trails crossing the whole country and all the Czech mountains.[[269]](#cite_note-275)[[270]](#cite_note-276)

## Explanatory notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Citizens belonging to minorities, which traditionally and on a long-term basis live within the territory of the Czech Republic, enjoy the right to use their language in communication with authorities and in courts of law (for the list of recognized minority languages, see [Minority languages of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_the_Czech_Republic "Minority languages of the Czech Republic")).[[2]](#cite_note-languages-2)
   Belarusian and Vietnamese since 4 July 2013.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Article 25 of the Czech [Charter of Fundamental Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_and_Freedoms "Charter of Fundamental Rights and Freedoms") ensures the right of the national and ethnic minorities to education and communication with the authorities in their own language. Act No. 500/2004 Coll. (*The Administrative Rule*) in its paragraph 16 (4) (*Procedural Language*) ensures that a citizen of the Czech Republic who belongs to a national or an ethnic minority, which traditionally and on a long-term basis lives within the territory of the Czech Republic, has the right to address an administrative agency and proceed before it in the language of the minority.[[4]](#cite_note-500_2004-4) If the administrative agency has no employee with knowledge of the language, the agency is bound to obtain a translator at the agency's own expense. According to Act No. 273/2001 (*Concerning the Rights of Members of Minorities*) paragraph 9 (*The right to use language of a national minority in dealing with authorities and in front of the courts of law*) the same also applies to members of national minorities in the courts of law.[[5]](#cite_note-report2004-5)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** The Slovak language may be considered an official language in the Czech Republic under certain circumstances, as defined by several laws.[[4]](#cite_note-500_2004-4)[[6]](#cite_note-7)
